EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed in the Nebraska Panhandle during the early evening hours of June 6th. As this activity tracked east, a broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed. Hail up to the size of ping pong balls, thunderstorm wind gusts to 70 MPH and a brief tornado touchdown were reported. Heavy rain also fell leading to flash flooding in western Keith county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail predominately penny size with some quarter size hail mixed in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With shallow cold air in place, warm advection associated with a weak wave of low pressure moving northeast from Kansas to Wisconsin produced a shield of light precipitation across northeast Nebraska. While most precipitation was in the form of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle on February 19 which resulted in a 0.05 to 0.10 inch ice accumulation, a second round of precipitation early on February 20 brought a dusting of less than an inch of snow which coated the ice and produced hazardous travel conditions. For the two day period, school delays were common for those not already out for the Washington's Birthday holiday.

With shallow cold air in place, warm advection associated with a weak wave of low pressure moving northeast from Kansas to Wisconsin produced a shield of light precipitation across northeast Nebraska. While most precipitation was in the form of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle on February 19 which resulted in a 0.05 to 0.10 inch ice accumulation, a second round of precipitation early on February 20 brought a dusting of less than an inch of snow which coated the ice and produced hazardous travel conditions. For the two day period, school delays were common for those not already out for the Washington's Birthday holiday.

Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota. It generally snowed for three to five hours at most across northeast Nebraska, briefly with visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts were less than 2 inches, but road conditions quickly became hazardous due to the rate of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of light to briefly moderate snowfall pushed across northeast Nebraska through early afternoon. Snowfall amounts were generally light, with 0.7 inch at Concord.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second disturbance in three days traversed the deep western trough, and the associated jet streak produced another area of snowfall across northeast Nebraska. Intensity waned for a short time in the late afternoon, allowing a brief change to light freezing drizzle. Snowfall varied from 1 to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle occurred at times between 1500CST and 1800CST, but mainly snowfall occurred resulting in an accumulation of 1.0 inch at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds on the backside of an upper level storm system reached the ground in Hitchcock and Red Willow counties.  The peak wind gust was 66 MPH at McCook.  The high winds also fueled a wildfire which consumed a house on the north side of McCook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high winds caused a piece of metal roofing to touch an overhead power line, igniting the fire three miles northwest of McCook.  The high winds pushed the fire in a southerly direction, burning 212 acres.  Evacuations were ordered for the northwest part of McCook totaling 446 people.  McCook elementary school students were moved to the high school.  Sixteen people were treated for burns and smoke inhalation.  The only severe burn victim was a transient living in a canyon.  One home on the north edge of McCook was destroyed by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough again progressed through the Rockies and evolved to a compact closed mid-level circulation drifting across eastern Nebraska and Iowa on April 18. Weak instability resulted in some scattered thunder with sleet and snow from the lower Missouri River valley through northwest Iowa. Marginally warmer temperatures aloft allowed for some light mixed precipitation early on April 18, but a quick transition to snow occurred in the pre-dawn hours, leaving behind between 2 and 6 inches snowfall across areas of northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation started in the early morning hours, but changed rapidly to snow after 0100CST. Snowfall totaled 5.2 inches at Newcastle and 3.0 inches at Concord.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snowfall beginning in the early morning hours on January 21st continuing into the morning hours on January 22nd across western and north central Nebraska. Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 12 inches across much of the area. Northwest winds increased to 20 to 35 mph during the afternoon and evening on January 21st, and into the morning hours on January 22nd. This caused blizzard conditions in some areas with considerable blowing and drifting, and visibility as low as a quarter mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts from 5 to 9 inches occurred across Holt County. Northwest winds of 20 to 35 mph, with gusts to 40 mph occurred during the evening hours on January 21st through the morning hours on January 22nd. Considerable blowing and drifting and visibility of a quarter mile or less occurred.  Snow drifts of 2 to 4 feet were reported. Schools were closed Monday. Some roads became closed. especially county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitter cold arctic air mass brought dangerous wind chills to portions of north central Nebraska during the overnight and morning hours on Sunday, December 31st, 2017 and continued to produce dangerous wind chills during the overnight and morning hours on Monday, January 1st, 2018. Lowest wind chills of 30 to 39 below were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous wind chills of 30 to 35 below zero occurred across eastern Cherry County during the overnight and morning hours on Monday, January 1st. The Valentine ASOS recorded a lowest wind chill of 35 below at 352 am CST while a mesonet station 14 miles north northeast of Brownlee recorded a lowest wind chill of 30 below at 614 am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough pushing through the region failed to phase a northern and southern stream component, resulting in limited and fairly light snowfall, but a period of strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just a dusting of light snowfall occurred over a 3 to 4 hour period after midnight on January 11, but with winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, visibility fell to a mile or less briefly creating travel difficulty. Strong wind gusts continued well through the daylight hours on January 11, with peak winds of 46 mph at Wayne and 52 mph at Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snowfall beginning in the early morning hours on January 21st continuing into the morning hours on January 22nd across western and north central Nebraska. Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 12 inches across much of the area. Northwest winds increased to 20 to 35 mph during the afternoon and evening on January 21st, and into the morning hours on January 22nd. This caused blizzard conditions in some areas with considerable blowing and drifting, and visibility as low as a quarter mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts from 4 to 9 inches occurred across Keith County. Northwest winds of 20 to 35 mph, with gusts to 45 mph occurred during the evening hours on January 21st through the early morning hours on January 22nd. Considerable blowing and drifting and visibility of a quarter mile or less at times occurred.  Snow drifts of 2 to 4 feet were reported. Schools were closed Monday. Some roads became closed, including a stretch of Interstate 80 between Brule to Big Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving clipper system deepened rapidly and carved out a closed upper level low across the western Great Lakes as it dropped southeast from the Canadian prairie provinces on the evening of January 14. The system produced an inch or less snowfall through January 15, as well as a brutal rush of arctic air into the region with winds gusting up to 35 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to 25 to 35 mph along with temperatures falling to subzero levels produced dangerous wind chills from -25 to -35. An inch of snowfall was measured at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills from -35 to -45 continued through the morning hours across northwest Iowa as a strong arctic surface ridge continued to slide into the Northern Plains. This continued the extreme cold which initiated during late December 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest winds at times from 6 to 11 mph combined with air temperatures around 20 below to 30 below zero to produce bitterly cold wind chills around -35 to -45 through the morning hours of New Year's Day. The actual air temperature plunged to a record -32 at Concord.  As southerly return flow developed behind the retreating ridge, not as extreme wind chills of -20 to -30 occurred from the evening of January 1st into mid morning of January 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Heavy snow and wind developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to Interstate 29 in eastern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain which produced a glaze of light icing during the early morning of December 1, heavy snow occurred for numerous hours. Accumulations over less than a 24-hour period totaled 8.0 inches at Emerson. Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce several hours of visibility of a half mile or less along with treacherous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27. A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN. This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28. A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN. ||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a period of light wintry mix, snow developed and winds increased across far southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, far northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska. Snowfall generally totaled 3 to 5 inches, with 4 to 6 inches near southern Sioux Falls. Other snowfall totals were 5.9 inches at Worthington MN, 5.0 inches at Canton SD, 4.0 inches near Vermillion SD, 3.5 inches near Larchwood IA, 3.0 inches at Luverne, MN and 2.3 inches at Concord NE. Visibility was reduced to a mile or less while heavier snowfall occurred, and winds were gusting to 37 mph at Slayton, 38 mph at Luverne and 46 mph at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains. As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm. Initially warm enough for rain, colder air rushed in as northwest winds rapidly increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph and brought a rapid change to snow late on January 21 and early January 22. Widespread visibility of a quarter mile or less with snow, heavy at times, continued through mid afternoon. Snow amassed to 8 to 13 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 0000CST January 22, with northwest winds increasing during the early morning to a gusty 35 to 45 mph. Frequent wind gusts along with heavy falling snow produced near zero visibility starting before daybreak, which continued into late afternoon. The Nebraska Dept. of Transportation pulled plows off roads around 0900CST. Winds peaked late morning, with reports of drifts up to 4 feet making roads impassible. Weather conditions gradually improved toward 1700CST, but travel was not advised well into Monday night. Total snowfall was measured at 12.5 inches at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blizzard conditions affected south central Nebraska on this Monday, with the worst conditions north of Interstate 80. On Sunday, a band of snow developed from northwest Kansas and extended northeast into the Nebraska Sandhills. This band formed in the deformation zone on the west side of the upper-level low and it remained stationary. The eastward fringe of this band affected areas from Cambridge to Lexington to Ord from 3 pm until 10 pm CST. Most of south central Nebraska was in the dry slot Sunday into Sunday evening. Multiple narrow bands of rain began forming over eastern Kansas, southeast Nebraska, and Iowa, east of the low Sunday evening. These bands rotated cyclonically, expanded and eventually merged with the larger snow band to the west which finally began moving east after midnight. By daybreak Monday, one large snow band extended from south central Nebraska into southern Minnesota. Over central Nebraska, the snow was heaviest and most persistent along and north of Highway 92, from 3 am to 9 am CST. At least 10 inches of snow fell over most of that area. The highest amounts were 14 inches at Belgrade and St. Paul. Greeley measured 12 inches, with 11 inches at Genoa, Osceola, and Shelby, and 10 inches at Ord. Other amounts of note include 7 inches at Grand Island and Ravenna. The southern tail of this band swept across the rest of south central Nebraska, but its narrow character resulted in much lower snow accumulations. One to 5 inches of snow fell from near Interstate 80 to the Kansas state line, with the lowest amounts near the state line. ||Strong winds developed with widespread gusts of 45 to 58 mph east of Highway 183. The highest wind gusts were 58 mph at Hastings and 57 mph at Cairo. This resulted in blizzard or near-blizzard conditions in many areas, but especially along and north of Interstate 80, with near white-out conditions reported. Conditions were so bad that the Nebraska Department of Transportation pulled their plows off the roads in Buffalo and Hall counties for about 2 hours. Numerous roads and highways were closed or reported as impassible, including portions of Highways 11, 58, and 92. Northbound Highway 281 was closed in Grand Island just north of Stolley Park Rd because two semi trucks could not make it over the overpass. Nebraska State Patrol responded to numerous vehicle accidents and vehicles that became stuck. Dozens of vehicles remained stranded as conditions were too dangerous for tow truck operators. The Polk county sheriff reported a number of cars slid into ditches on Highway 81. Portions of Interstate 80 in York county were closed for a couple hours due to multiple accidents. Power lines were reported down in Phelps county, with isolated power outages also reported in and near Lexington and from Franklin county northeast to Hamilton and Merrick counties. Numerous schools, government offices, and businesses were closed Monday, as well as the regional airports in Grand Island and Kearney. The University of Nebraska at Kearney, Hastings College, and all Central Community Colleges at Lexington, Ord, Kearney, Hastings, and Grand Island were also closed. Snow emergencies were declared in Aurora, Cozad, Gibbon, Gothenburg, Grand Island, Kearney, Hastings, Holdrege, Minden, and St. Paul. A winter storm produced heavy snowfall beginning in the early morning hours on January 21st continuing into the morning hours on January 22nd across western and north central Nebraska. Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 12 inches across much of the area. Northwest winds increased to 20 to 35 mph during the afternoon and evening on January 21st, and into the morning hours on January 22nd. This caused blizzard conditions in some areas with considerable blowing and drifting, and visibility as low as a quarter mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts from 9 to 13 inches occurred across Lincoln County. Northwest winds of 20 to 35 mph, with gusts to 45 mph occurred during the evening hours on January 21st through the early morning hours on January 22nd. Considerable blowing and drifting and visibility of a quarter mile or less occurred.  Snow drifts of 2 to 5 feet were reported. Schools were closed Monday. A portion of highway 83 was closed near Wellfleet, as well as numerous county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitter cold arctic air mass brought dangerous wind chills to portions of north central Nebraska during the overnight and morning hours on Sunday, December 31st, 2017 and continued to produce dangerous wind chills during the overnight and morning hours on Monday, January 1st, 2018. Lowest wind chills of 30 to 39 below were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous wind chills of 30 to 35 below zero occurred across Keya Paha County during the overnight and morning hours on Monday, January 1st. The nearby Valentine ASOS recorded a lowest wind chill of 35 below at 352 am CST while the nearby Ainsworth AWOS recorded a lowest wind chill of 32 below at 515 am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate to heavy snow moved east from Colorado.  Under the band of snow the visibility fell to zero.  During the night the first band gradually ended as a second band formed and continued east, also causing visibility to fall to near zero under it.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 9 to 13 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county was around 10 inches.  Duration of the wind gusts during the storm were below blizzard criteria.  However blizzard like conditions were observed.

Although not a major storm by any means, this Sunday morning featured a quick-hitting burst of fluffy, wind-driven snow that roughly doubled accumulation expectations across much of South Central Nebraska, with several spots receiving 2-4 (and locally higher). The vast majority of 2+ amounts focused southwest of a line from Loup City-Grand Island-Clay Center-Hebron. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 5 at Red Cloud, 4 at Hubbell, 3.9 at Hebron and also four miles southwest of Blue Hill, and 3.8 eight miles south of Elwood. In the Nebraska Tri Cities, totals included: 1.7 at Grand Island airport, 2.6 at Hastings NWS and 2.9 at Kearney airport. Although even the higher-end totals were relatively modest, this event packed somewhat of a punch given sustained north winds generally 20-25 MPH/gusts 30+ MPH, resulting in some drifting and at least brief visibility reductions to one-half mile or less. ||As for timing, the vast majority of accumulating snow fell between 3-10 AM, gradually ending from north-to-south as forcing departed southward into Kansas. Speaking of forcing, this event was driven by a classic case of pronounced mid-level frontogenesis, trailing well-behind a strong surface cold front that passed through during the preceding 24 hours. On the larger scale, a broad shortwave trough tracked southeast from the Northern Rockies/Dakotas into the Midwest over the course of Feb. 4th. Locally, this snow event was accompanied by a very cold airmass featuring surface temperatures only in the single digits above zero F, and wind chill values ranging from -10 to -20 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Miller reported 3.1 inches of total snowfall. Also, an official snowfall total of 2.9 inches was measured at the Kearney Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the afternoon of Friday the 9th and the evening of Saturday the 10th, two completely separate rounds of Advisory-worthy snow affected parts of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area, making for an active couple days of winter weather despite only modest impacts. Interestingly, the two snow events overlapped minimally in area, with the first round focused mainly within northern and central counties, and the second one across the south. This event summary discusses round 1, which occurred between the afternoon of the 9th and the pre-dawn hours of the 10th:||This first round of snow primarily affected areas north of Highway 6, and especially counties along and north of Interstate 80, which reported a fairly widespread coverage of 2-4 and isolated higher amounts to around 5. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 5 northwest of Greeley, 4.7 west of Arcadia, 4.2 at Grand Island airport and 3.6 at Ravenna. Other official Tri Cities totals included 2.3 at Kearney and only 1.7 at the Hastings NWS office. Although not overly-strong, sustained north winds averaged 10-20 MPH with some higher gusts, causing at least minor blowing and drifting of the fluffy snow. Timing-wise, while some northern counties received minor accumulation early in the afternoon of the 9th, the primary west-east oriented snow band got going in the north between 4-6 PM before gradually slipping southward across the Interstate 80 corridor through the evening. Between midnight and 2 AM on the 10th, this snow band weakened and shifted east of the local area, signaling the end of round 1. Aloft, a large-scale, broad trough was in place over much of the United States with a series of jet streaks passing directly over Nebraska. This setup yielded a concentrated swath of mid-level frontogenesis, which in turn promoted the rather narrow, west-east oriented snow band that dropped the majority of accumulation. At the surface, the fluffy nature of the flakes was supported by a very cold airmass with temperatures only in the single digits above zero F. The breezy north winds resulted from steady pressure rises emanating southward across the Central Plains from high pressure in Montana and the Dakotas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 2.7 inches occurred in St. Paul.

The heaviest amounts included 2.7 inches at 68th and Dodge in Omaha, 3 inches west of Oakland, 4 inches at Scribner, NE , and 5 inches southwest of Dunlap, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2.7 inches of snow reported at 62nd Street and Dodge in Omaha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate to heavy snowfall slowly moved south across Dundy County during the day. Snowfall reports ranged from four to nine inches, with the highest amount reported north of Parks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from four to nine inches, with the higher amounts of snow occurring in the central part of the county extending from east to west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance rotating around a large Canadian upper vortex brought a period of enhanced frontogenetic forcing through the Missouri River valley and eastward across northwest Iowa and far southern Minnesota. Snowfall averaged from 2 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow over a period of several hours brought from 2 to 5 inches of snowfall across northeast Nebraska. Higher amounts were measured at Concord (3.9 inches) and Emerson (3.0 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in three days, South Central Nebraska saw a modest-but-noticeable round of snow on this Tuesday, as most of the 24-county area received 1-3, and localized totals to 4. Unlike the event two days prior on the 4th, winds were not much of a factor, with southeasterly speeds only averaging around 10 MPH. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 4 at Geneva and four miles east of Ohiowa, 3.9 at Ravenna, 3.7 eight miles south of Elwood and 3.6 two miles northwest of Rockville. In the Nebraska Tri Cities, official totals included 3 at both Kearney and Grand Island airports and 2 at the Hastings NWS office. ||This event was squarely centered on the daytime hours, as snow first broke out in far southwestern counties (such as Furnas) shortly before sunrise and gradually expanded northeastward, affecting the majority of South Central Nebraska by mid-late morning. As the afternoon and early evening wore on, snow slowly ended from west-to-east, with the final steady band exiting Fillmore/Thayer counties between 7-8 PM. In the mid-upper levels, the primary instigator was a shortwave trough migrating east out of Colorado across Kansas and far southern Nebraska. At the surface, this was another very cold snow with daytime highs only topping out around 10 F, and the light winds promoted by strong Arctic high pressure (roughly 1033 millibars) centered over Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.7 inches occurred 8 miles south of Elwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread high winds occurred on this Monday and Tuesday. Between midnight and 6 AM CST Monday, an occluded front swept across south central Nebraska. During the day Monday, several lows consolidated into a primary low along the South Dakota-Minnesota border. This low became nearly stationary as the fronts continued to progress far to the east and south. Monday night this low performed a loop and moved southeast into Iowa. During the day Tuesday, this low rapidly filled and became absorbed into a low over the Great Lakes. Two periods of strong winds occurred. The first occurred from midday through the afternoon hours Monday due to diurnal mixing. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 61 mph occurred mainly along and west of Highway 281. There were a couple mesonet sites that measured 64 mph near Kearney and 69 mph near Norman, although these stations may not necessarily be sited properly. From the official stations, the highest winds were measured at Lexington where winds were sustained at or above 40 mph for five hours with a peak gust of 61 mph. The high winds combined with dry fuels and relative humidity levels as low as 23% to create favorable conditions for the wildfire that occurred near Wilsonville. There was a lull over much of the area during the evening, then winds increased again. The second period of high winds occurred between midnight and 9 AM CST on Tuesday. These winds were associated with a tightening of the pressure gradient as the low performed its loop. This also induced steepening lapse rates associated with an increase in cold air advection. Widespread wind gusts between 50 and 68 mph occurred. The highest wind gusts from official stations included 66 mph at the airport in Hastings and 65 mph at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. The highest unofficial gust was 68 mph near Norman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a wildfire near Wilsonville in Furnas County during the mid-late afternoon hours, located near the intersection of Highway 89 and Road 414, north of Ag Valley COOP. Emergency manager said this was started by with the wind and a rekindled burn permit from a few weeks ago, and burned approximately 350 acres. Fire departments from Wilsonville, Cambridge, Bartley, Beaver Valley (Lebanon/Danbury), HEA (Holbrook/Arapahoe/Edison), Beaver City and Norton County, KS were on the scene. Ambulances from Cambridge and Beaver City were present for rehab. Extreme Ag (Cambridge), Ag Valley COOP and Kasson Farms provided extra trucks to haul water. There were also 5-6 tractors with discs/dozer blades to provide assistance. This fire burned grassland, no structures were lost.

Although even the higher-end totals were relatively modest, this event packed somewhat of a punch given sustained north winds generally 20-25 MPH/gusts 30+ MPH, resulting in some drifting and at least brief visibility reductions to one-half mile or less. ||As for timing, the vast majority of accumulating snow fell between 3-10 AM, gradually ending from north-to-south as forcing departed southward into Kansas. Speaking of forcing, this event was driven by a classic case of pronounced mid-level frontogenesis, trailing well-behind a strong surface cold front that passed through during the preceding 24 hours. On the larger scale, a broad shortwave trough tracked southeast from the Northern Rockies/Dakotas into the Midwest over the course of Feb. 4th. Locally, this snow event was accompanied by a very cold airmass featuring surface temperatures only in the single digits above zero F, and wind chill values ranging from -10 to -20 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.0 inches occurred in Clay Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in three days, South Central Nebraska saw a modest-but-noticeable round of snow on this Tuesday, as most of the 24-county area received 1-3, and localized totals to 4. Unlike the event two days prior on the 4th, winds were not much of a factor, with southeasterly speeds only averaging around 10 MPH. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 4 at Geneva and four miles east of Ohiowa, 3.9 at Ravenna, 3.7 eight miles south of Elwood and 3.6 two miles northwest of Rockville. In the Nebraska Tri Cities, official totals included 3 at both Kearney and Grand Island airports and 2 at the Hastings NWS office. ||This event was squarely centered on the daytime hours, as snow first broke out in far southwestern counties (such as Furnas) shortly before sunrise and gradually expanded northeastward, affecting the majority of South Central Nebraska by mid-late morning. As the afternoon and early evening wore on, snow slowly ended from west-to-east, with the final steady band exiting Fillmore/Thayer counties between 7-8 PM. In the mid-upper levels, the primary instigator was a shortwave trough migrating east out of Colorado across Kansas and far southern Nebraska. At the surface, this was another very cold snow with daytime highs only topping out around 10 F, and the light winds promoted by strong Arctic high pressure (roughly 1033 millibars) centered over Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.0 inches occurred in St. Paul.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the afternoon of Friday the 9th and the evening of Saturday the 10th, two completely separate rounds of Advisory-worthy snow affected parts of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area, making for an active couple days of winter weather despite only modest impacts. Interestingly, the two snow events overlapped minimally in area, with the first round focused mainly within northern and central counties, and the second one across the south. This event summary discusses round 1, which occurred between the afternoon of the 9th and the pre-dawn hours of the 10th:||This first round of snow primarily affected areas north of Highway 6, and especially counties along and north of Interstate 80, which reported a fairly widespread coverage of 2-4 and isolated higher amounts to around 5. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 5 northwest of Greeley, 4.7 west of Arcadia, 4.2 at Grand Island airport and 3.6 at Ravenna. Other official Tri Cities totals included 2.3 at Kearney and only 1.7 at the Hastings NWS office. Although not overly-strong, sustained north winds averaged 10-20 MPH with some higher gusts, causing at least minor blowing and drifting of the fluffy snow. Timing-wise, while some northern counties received minor accumulation early in the afternoon of the 9th, the primary west-east oriented snow band got going in the north between 4-6 PM before gradually slipping southward across the Interstate 80 corridor through the evening. Between midnight and 2 AM on the 10th, this snow band weakened and shifted east of the local area, signaling the end of round 1. Aloft, a large-scale, broad trough was in place over much of the United States with a series of jet streaks passing directly over Nebraska. This setup yielded a concentrated swath of mid-level frontogenesis, which in turn promoted the rather narrow, west-east oriented snow band that dropped the majority of accumulation. At the surface, the fluffy nature of the flakes was supported by a very cold airmass with temperatures only in the single digits above zero F. The breezy north winds resulted from steady pressure rises emanating southward across the Central Plains from high pressure in Montana and the Dakotas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.5 inches was reported from CoCoRaHS 3 miles north northeast of Johnson Lake. And also, a public report of 3.0 inches in Gothenburg was reported via Facebook.

Other official Tri Cities totals included 2.3 at Kearney and only 1.7 at the Hastings NWS office. Although not overly-strong, sustained north winds averaged 10-20 MPH with some higher gusts, causing at least minor blowing and drifting of the fluffy snow. Timing-wise, while some northern counties received minor accumulation early in the afternoon of the 9th, the primary west-east oriented snow band got going in the north between 4-6 PM before gradually slipping southward across the Interstate 80 corridor through the evening. This setup yielded a concentrated swath of mid-level frontogenesis, which in turn promoted the rather narrow, west-east oriented snow band that dropped the majority of accumulation. At the surface, the fluffy nature of the flakes was supported by a very cold airmass with temperatures only in the single digits above zero F. The breezy north winds resulted from steady pressure rises emanating southward across the Central Plains from high pressure in Montana and the Dakotas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in three days, South Central Nebraska saw a modest-but-noticeable round of snow on this Tuesday, as most of the 24-county area received 1-3, and localized totals to 4. Unlike the event two days prior on the 4th, winds were not much of a factor, with southeasterly speeds only averaging around 10 MPH. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 4 at Geneva and four miles east of Ohiowa, 3.9 at Ravenna, 3.7 eight miles south of Elwood and 3.6 two miles northwest of Rockville. In the Nebraska Tri Cities, official totals included 3 at both Kearney and Grand Island airports and 2 at the Hastings NWS office. ||This event was squarely centered on the daytime hours, as snow first broke out in far southwestern counties (such as Furnas) shortly before sunrise and gradually expanded northeastward, affecting the majority of South Central Nebraska by mid-late morning. As the afternoon and early evening wore on, snow slowly ended from west-to-east, with the final steady band exiting Fillmore/Thayer counties between 7-8 PM. In the mid-upper levels, the primary instigator was a shortwave trough migrating east out of Colorado across Kansas and far southern Nebraska. At the surface, this was another very cold snow with daytime highs only topping out around 10 F, and the light winds promoted by strong Arctic high pressure (roughly 1033 millibars) centered over Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.0 inches occurred 3 miles west of Gresham, while 2.5 inches occurred 3  miles north of York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of Alberta clipper systems moving across the plains left behind a swath of light to moderate snow accumulations across northeast Nebraska. Much of the area received from 3 to 5 inches snowfall, while wind chills dropped below zero by the end of the time of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, producing hazardous travel conditions with snowfall totaling as much as 4.3 inches at Newcastle and 1.5 inches at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although not a major storm by any means, this Sunday morning featured a quick-hitting burst of fluffy, wind-driven snow that roughly doubled accumulation expectations across much of South Central Nebraska, with several spots receiving 2-4 (and locally higher). The vast majority of 2+ amounts focused southwest of a line from Loup City-Grand Island-Clay Center-Hebron. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 5 at Red Cloud, 4 at Hubbell, 3.9 at Hebron and also four miles southwest of Blue Hill, and 3.8 eight miles south of Elwood. In the Nebraska Tri Cities, totals included: 1.7 at Grand Island airport, 2.6 at Hastings NWS and 2.9 at Kearney airport. Although even the higher-end totals were relatively modest, this event packed somewhat of a punch given sustained north winds generally 20-25 MPH/gusts 30+ MPH, resulting in some drifting and at least brief visibility reductions to one-half mile or less. ||As for timing, the vast majority of accumulating snow fell between 3-10 AM, gradually ending from north-to-south as forcing departed southward into Kansas. Speaking of forcing, this event was driven by a classic case of pronounced mid-level frontogenesis, trailing well-behind a strong surface cold front that passed through during the preceding 24 hours. On the larger scale, a broad shortwave trough tracked southeast from the Northern Rockies/Dakotas into the Midwest over the course of Feb. 4th. Locally, this snow event was accompanied by a very cold airmass featuring surface temperatures only in the single digits above zero F, and wind chill values ranging from -10 to -20 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.8 inches occurred 8 miles south of Elwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another fast moving upper level weather system brought several bands of snow across portions of east central and southeast Nebraska, and parts of southwest Iowa during the afternoon and evening of February 6th.  Most areas received 1-3 inches of snow.  A band of heavier snow brought 4 to 5 inches to parts of Saline County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Odell, 3 inches of snow was measured.  At Pickrell, 3.2 inches of snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first severe thunderstorms of the season occurred over Furnas and Harlan counties on this Friday evening. Scattered showers and a few elevated thunderstorms crossed these counties and adjacent areas between 5 PM and 6:30 PM CST. These storms were not severe. Meanwhile, a larger area of showers developed over southwest Nebraska. Around 6 PM CST, a thunderstorm formed at the southern edge of these showers south of McCook. Over the next two hours, this storm expanded into a small cyclonic-shaped arc of thunderstorms. This middle portion of this arc moved east across Furnas and Harlan counties between 6:30 and 8 PM CST. A small number of isolated, low-end severe hail reports were received. A few hailstones the size of quarters were observed in Edison. This was the largest hail size reported.||Cyclogenesis was occurring over eastern Colorado during the morning hours. As the low moved into western Kansas in the afternoon, a front that had been stationary from northwest to southeast across Kansas began moving north. By the time the storms formed, the front had moved to the Nebraska-Kansas border. The portion of this short line of storms that affected Furnas and Harlan counties was just north of the front. Strong cold air advection was occurring which lowered temperatures to near 50 with dewpoints in the upper 40s. The cyclonic arc shape was to due the storms forming within a ribbon of mid-level frontogenesis. An elevated mixed layer had overspread the area, but instability was weak with most unstable CAPE between 700 and 900 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 60 kt.

Although not a major storm by any means, this Sunday morning featured a quick-hitting burst of fluffy, wind-driven snow that roughly doubled accumulation expectations across much of South Central Nebraska, with several spots receiving 2-4 (and locally higher). The vast majority of 2+ amounts focused southwest of a line from Loup City-Grand Island-Clay Center-Hebron. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 5 at Red Cloud, 4 at Hubbell, 3.9 at Hebron and also four miles southwest of Blue Hill, and 3.8 eight miles south of Elwood. In the Nebraska Tri Cities, totals included: 1.7 at Grand Island airport, 2.6 at Hastings NWS and 2.9 at Kearney airport. Although even the higher-end totals were relatively modest, this event packed somewhat of a punch given sustained north winds generally 20-25 MPH/gusts 30+ MPH, resulting in some drifting and at least brief visibility reductions to one-half mile or less. ||As for timing, the vast majority of accumulating snow fell between 3-10 AM, gradually ending from north-to-south as forcing departed southward into Kansas. Speaking of forcing, this event was driven by a classic case of pronounced mid-level frontogenesis, trailing well-behind a strong surface cold front that passed through during the preceding 24 hours. On the larger scale, a broad shortwave trough tracked southeast from the Northern Rockies/Dakotas into the Midwest over the course of Feb. 4th. Locally, this snow event was accompanied by a very cold airmass featuring surface temperatures only in the single digits above zero F, and wind chill values ranging from -10 to -20 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.0 inches occurred in Wilsonville. Also, 2.4 inches occurred 6 miles north northwest of Oxford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another fast moving upper level weather system brought several bands of snow across portions of east central and southeast Nebraska, and parts of southwest Iowa during the afternoon and evening of February 6th.  Most areas received 1-3 inches of snow.  A band of heavier snow brought 4 to 5 inches to parts of Saline County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report of 1.5 inches of snow measured at Waverly. And 1.8 inches of snow fell  2 miles south southwest of Lincoln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet pushing across the Rockies resulted in development of low pressure across southeast Colorado late on April 2, which intensified rapidly moving across Kansas and into Illinois on April 3. A broad swath of snow and gusty winds developed north of the low track across the area, with heaviest snowfall amounts in excess of 6 inches generally corresponding to highest terrain areas in south central and southeast South Dakota, northern portions of northwest Iowa and areas of southwest Minnesota. As unseasonably cold high pressure spread in behind the system on the morning of April 4, a few low temperature records were broken by as much as 5 to 15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of snowfall developed in the early morning hours of April 3, continuing at times into early afternoon. Winds shifted northwest and increased substantially with gusts of 30 to 40 mph at times through the late morning and afternoon. Snowfall totaled 3.8 inches at Concord.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most intense storm of the month, in fact many months, wrapped up across the central Plains on April 13, spreading a mix of wintry precipitation across the area, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms. Not included in this episode was the spotty severe hail and wind which occurred in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota prior to the onset of snowfall during the morning and afternoon of April 13. |Eventually, influx of colder air would change all precipitation over to snow, and ushered in a record-breaking snowfall of 8 to 16 inches (some locations over 20 inches) and fierce winds gusting 50 to 60 mph to southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa and extreme northeast Nebraska. Snow drifts over 6 feet were reported in many locations. Extremely low visibility persisted for almost a day for most locations, making travel very difficult if not impossible. ||One person was killed in Rock County when the snowmobile he was riding collided with an interstate guard rail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life threatening conditions developed during the early morning of April 14, as the earlier mix of rain, sleet and snow changed to all snow, and brutal winds (gusting as high as 46 mph at Sioux City) whipped visibility to less than a quarter mile at times through the evening of April 14.  Travel was not recommended for much of the two day period, if not impossible. A storm total snowfall of 4.5 inches was measured at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread high winds occurred on this Monday and Tuesday. Between midnight and 6 AM CST Monday, an occluded front swept across south central Nebraska. During the day Monday, several lows consolidated into a primary low along the South Dakota-Minnesota border. This low became nearly stationary as the fronts continued to progress far to the east and south. Monday night this low performed a loop and moved southeast into Iowa. During the day Tuesday, this low rapidly filled and became absorbed into a low over the Great Lakes. Two periods of strong winds occurred. The first occurred from midday through the afternoon hours Monday due to diurnal mixing. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 61 mph occurred mainly along and west of Highway 281. There were a couple mesonet sites that measured 64 mph near Kearney and 69 mph near Norman, although these stations may not necessarily be sited properly. From the official stations, the highest winds were measured at Lexington where winds were sustained at or above 40 mph for five hours with a peak gust of 61 mph. The high winds combined with dry fuels and relative humidity levels as low as 23% to create favorable conditions for the wildfire that occurred near Wilsonville. There was a lull over much of the area during the evening, then winds increased again. The second period of high winds occurred between midnight and 9 AM CST on Tuesday. These winds were associated with a tightening of the pressure gradient as the low performed its loop. This also induced steepening lapse rates associated with an increase in cold air advection. Widespread wind gusts between 50 and 68 mph occurred. The highest wind gusts from official stations included 66 mph at the airport in Hastings and 65 mph at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. The highest unofficial gust was 68 mph near Norman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by the Ord Airport ASOS.

During the day Tuesday, this low rapidly filled and became absorbed into a low over the Great Lakes. Two periods of strong winds occurred. The first occurred from midday through the afternoon hours Monday due to diurnal mixing. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 61 mph occurred mainly along and west of Highway 281. There were a couple mesonet sites that measured 64 mph near Kearney and 69 mph near Norman, although these stations may not necessarily be sited properly. From the official stations, the highest winds were measured at Lexington where winds were sustained at or above 40 mph for five hours with a peak gust of 61 mph. The high winds combined with dry fuels and relative humidity levels as low as 23% to create favorable conditions for the wildfire that occurred near Wilsonville. There was a lull over much of the area during the evening, then winds increased again. The second period of high winds occurred between midnight and 9 AM CST on Tuesday. These winds were associated with a tightening of the pressure gradient as the low performed its loop. This also induced steepening lapse rates associated with an increase in cold air advection. Widespread wind gusts between 50 and 68 mph occurred. The highest wind gusts from official stations included 66 mph at the airport in Hastings and 65 mph at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. The highest unofficial gust was 68 mph near Norman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 68 MPH was measured by a mesonet located 2 miles north-northeast of Norman.

This low became nearly stationary as the fronts continued to progress far to the east and south. Monday night this low performed a loop and moved southeast into Iowa. During the day Tuesday, this low rapidly filled and became absorbed into a low over the Great Lakes. Two periods of strong winds occurred. The first occurred from midday through the afternoon hours Monday due to diurnal mixing. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 61 mph occurred mainly along and west of Highway 281. There were a couple mesonet sites that measured 64 mph near Kearney and 69 mph near Norman, although these stations may not necessarily be sited properly. From the official stations, the highest winds were measured at Lexington where winds were sustained at or above 40 mph for five hours with a peak gust of 61 mph. The high winds combined with dry fuels and relative humidity levels as low as 23% to create favorable conditions for the wildfire that occurred near Wilsonville. There was a lull over much of the area during the evening, then winds increased again. The second period of high winds occurred between midnight and 9 AM CST on Tuesday. These winds were associated with a tightening of the pressure gradient as the low performed its loop. This also induced steepening lapse rates associated with an increase in cold air advection. Widespread wind gusts between 50 and 68 mph occurred. The highest wind gusts from official stations included 66 mph at the airport in Hastings and 65 mph at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. The highest unofficial gust was 68 mph near Norman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by the Ord Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across portions of Dawson, Gosper, Phelps, and Kearney counties on this Monday. During the evening hours on the 18th, a cyclonic band of precipitation developed east and north of the developing mid-level low. This band of precipitation remained over Kansas. However, a large area of snow developed north of this band over south central Nebraska primarily south of Interstate 80. The northern fringe remained stationary for a time as it pivoted between midnight and 6 AM. The snow then ended as it dropped to the south. The highest amount was 5.5 inches at Johnson Lake. Other amounts include 5 inches at Holdrege, 4 at Minden, and 3-4 inches in and near Lexington.||Lee cyclogenesis occurred over Colorado the day before and this low moved east along the Kansas-Oklahoma border that night. A strong trough progressed east from Colorado and New Mexico with a closed mid-level low forming on the Kansas-Oklahoma state line. This snow developed in a weak area of frontogenesis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought areas of light freezing rain, followed by a period of heavy snowfall to much of Sheridan County and Cherry County during the morning into the afternoon hours. Freezing rain around a tenth of an inch with local amounts up to a quarter inch were reported. Snowfall amounts from 4 to 6 inches were also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General public reported via social media, 4.6 inches of snowfall at 410 pm cst. The Valentine ASOS also recorded a quarter inch of ice accumulation at 1252 pm cst. Snowfall amounts from 3 to 6 inches, and ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch were common across eastern Cherry County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of Colorado across east central Nebraska and produced snow and strong winds across northeast Nebraska. This created blowing snow conditions | and whiteout conditions across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Winds gusted to 55 mph at Lincoln, NE, 53 mph at Norfolk, and 47 mph at  Eppley Airfield in Omaha. Snowfall ranged from about 1 inch in Lincoln and Columbus to as much as 6 inches in northern Cedar and Knox Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Thurston County Emergency Manager reported 2.5 inches of snow in Pender.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This began the first of many high wind days for March. The high winds from an upper level low that did reach the ground were very sparse across Red Willow County. The peak wind gust was 61 MPH at McCook.

This began the first of many high wind days for March. The high winds from an upper level low that did reach the ground were very sparse across Red Willow County. The peak wind gust was 61 MPH at McCook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 59 mph were reported by Dundy County High School in Benkelman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon a strong line of thunderstorm developed over Red Willow County ahead of a cold front, producing nickel size hail near Danbury.  The stronger part of the line was south of the state line in Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail lasted 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although not a major storm by any means, this Sunday morning featured a quick-hitting burst of fluffy, wind-driven snow that roughly doubled accumulation expectations across much of South Central Nebraska, with several spots receiving 2-4 (and locally higher). The vast majority of 2+ amounts focused southwest of a line from Loup City-Grand Island-Clay Center-Hebron. According to reports from NeRAIN/CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 5 at Red Cloud, 4 at Hubbell, 3.9 at Hebron and also four miles southwest of Blue Hill, and 3.8 eight miles south of Elwood. In the Nebraska Tri Cities, totals included: 1.7 at Grand Island airport, 2.6 at Hastings NWS and 2.9 at Kearney airport. Although even the higher-end totals were relatively modest, this event packed somewhat of a punch given sustained north winds generally 20-25 MPH/gusts 30+ MPH, resulting in some drifting and at least brief visibility reductions to one-half mile or less. ||As for timing, the vast majority of accumulating snow fell between 3-10 AM, gradually ending from north-to-south as forcing departed southward into Kansas. Speaking of forcing, this event was driven by a classic case of pronounced mid-level frontogenesis, trailing well-behind a strong surface cold front that passed through during the preceding 24 hours. On the larger scale, a broad shortwave trough tracked southeast from the Northern Rockies/Dakotas into the Midwest over the course of Feb. 4th. Locally, this snow event was accompanied by a very cold airmass featuring surface temperatures only in the single digits above zero F, and wind chill values ranging from -10 to -20 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 5.0 inches occurred in Red Cloud. Also, 3.9 inches of snowfall occurred 4 miles southwest of Blue Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sunday the 29th and Monday the 30th were two of the windiest consecutive days of spring 2018 within South Central Nebraska, especially in terms of SOUTH winds. On both days, primarily the afternoon featured several hours of sustained speeds commonly 25-35 MPH (isolated 40 MPH) and frequent gusts to around 50 MPH, along with isolated severe-criteria gusts of 58+ MPH. Also on both days, the combination of recent dry conditions and recently-tilled fields promoted a number of localized dust storms, causing drastic visibility reductions over short distances. Specifically on the late afternoon of the 29th, dust blowing across Interstate 80 near the Aurora exit led to a major pileup of 29 vehicles, closing I-80 for over two hours. Sadly, this incident resulted in one fatality and over a dozen injuries. ||As for measured winds from a variety of official and unofficial sources, a few peak gusts on the 29th featured: 62 MPH near Kearney (mesonet), 59 MPH near Oxford (mesonet), and 58 MPH at Grand Island and Ord airports. At Kearney Regional Airport, sustained winds averaged 40 MPH for an hour, mainly between 12-1 PM CDT. Although it remained quite breezy even after dark, gusts by late evening largely eased below 40 MPH. Turning to the meteorological background, this was a classic recipe for prolonged, strong south winds across the Central Plains. In the mid-upper levels, an expansive, large-scale trough gradually deepened over the western CONUS, only edging slowly eastward. This in turn promoted strengthening surface low pressure across the Central High Plains, averaging around 998 millibars in eastern Colorado. This low pressure, in concert with sprawling high pressure centered over the eastern CONUS, set up a healthy pressure gradient. During the height of the strongest winds on the 29th, the state of Nebraska featured roughly a 16-millibar gradient, ranging from around 1000 millibars in the western Panhandle, to around 1016 millibars in far southeast counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a peak gust of 60 MPH on the east side of Kearney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sunday the 29th and Monday the 30th were two of the windiest consecutive days of spring 2018 within South Central Nebraska, especially in terms of SOUTH winds. On both days, primarily the afternoon featured several hours of sustained speeds commonly 25-35 MPH (isolated 40 MPH) and frequent gusts to around 50 MPH, along with isolated severe-criteria gusts of 58+ MPH. Also on both days, the combination of recent dry conditions and recently-tilled fields promoted a number of localized dust storms, causing drastic visibility reductions over short distances. Specifically on the afternoon of the 30th, dust blowing across Interstate 80 led to another multi-vehicle pileup, but this one occurred just east of the local South Central Nebraska area in Seward County, and was not quite as severe as the one the previous day in Hamilton County. Although strong south winds and blowing dust were clearly the main stories on the 30th, the far northern fringe of the local area (northwestern Valley County) was brushed by a line of strong to severe thunderstorms mainly between 9-11 PM CDT, yielding a report of quarter size hail west-southwest of Elyria, along with nearly 3 inches of rain. ||As for measured winds from a variety of official and unofficial sources, some peak gusts on the 30th featured: 60 MPH near Bradshaw (mesonet), 58 MPH near Nelson and Davenport (mesonets), and 57 MPH at Grand Island airport. Like the preceding day, gusts largely eased below 40 MPH after dark. Turning to the meteorological background, this was a classic recipe for prolonged, strong south winds across the Central Plains. In the mid-upper levels, an expansive, large-scale trough gradually deepened over the western CONUS, only edging slowly eastward. This in turn promoted strengthening surface low pressure across the Central High Plains, averaging around 998 millibars in eastern Colorado. This low pressure, in concert with sprawling high pressure centered over the eastern CONUS, set up a healthy pressure gradient. Looking a bit closer at the severe storm that affected Valley County during the evening, this activity evolved eastward from convection that initially formed just west of the local area along a slow-moving cold front. Mesoscale parameters in the area featured mixed-layer CAPE at least 1000 J/kg and deep-layer wind shear around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet near Bradshaw gusted to 60 MPH.

A winter storm produced blizzard conditions across western and north central Nebraska. Snow developed and winds intensified Friday morning, in the panhandle and western Sandhills, then spread east through the evening and overnight hours, where the snow was mixed with sleet at times. On Friday, thunderstorms developed with dime sized hail being reported in Holt county.  The blizzard conditions continued Saturday morning, with conditions improving Saturday afternoon, as the snow and winds diminished. The heaviest snow occurred from the eastern panhandle into west central and north central Nebraska. These areas received 6 to 10 inches, with locally higher amounts of 12 to 14 inches. Strong northwest winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts from 50 to 60 mph at times, beginning Friday afternoon and continuing Friday night into Saturday morning. This created blizzard conditions and resulted in snow drifts several feet high and multiple highway closures, including Interstate 80.

Thunderstorms developed along a surface front over southern South Dakota during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 8th. The storms merged into a bow-echo complex which tracked southeast through eastern Nebraska during the early-morning hours of June 9th. The thunderstorms produced wind damage and a measured wind gust of 74 mph at the Lincoln Municipal Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eight inch diameter tree was snapped in half near Sapp Brothers on Cornhusker Highway in northeast Lincoln.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person estimated 70 mph winds  5 miles south southeast of Creston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level trough of low pressure, forced a cold front through western and north central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 30th. Thunderstorms initiated along a line from western Frontier county, north northeast into Blaine County. Further development occurred east of this line into the late evening hours. Hail up to the size of golf balls along with thunderstorm wind gusts to 65 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced blizzard conditions across western and north central Nebraska. Snow developed and winds intensified Friday morning, in the panhandle and western Sandhills, then spread east through the evening and overnight hours, where the snow was mixed with sleet at times. On Friday, thunderstorms developed with dime sized hail being reported in Holt county.  The blizzard conditions continued Saturday morning, with conditions improving Saturday afternoon, as the snow and winds diminished. The heaviest snow occurred from the eastern panhandle into west central and north central Nebraska. These areas received 6 to 10 inches, with locally higher amounts of 12 to 14 inches. Strong northwest winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts from 50 to 60 mph at times, beginning Friday afternoon and continuing Friday night into Saturday morning. This created blizzard conditions and resulted in snow drifts several feet high and multiple highway closures, including Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative observer in Arthur reported 8 inches of snowfall.  Moderate to heavy snow developed Friday morning as northwest winds increased late Friday morning to 30 to 40 mph with gusts from 50 to 60 mph, resulting in blizzard conditions. These strong winds and blizzard conditions continued Friday afternoon and Friday night, then diminished Saturday morning.  Highways 92 and 61 became closed, with snow drifts of 3 to 5 feet common throughout Arthur County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced blizzard conditions across western and north central Nebraska. Snow developed and winds intensified Friday morning, in the panhandle and western Sandhills, then spread east through the evening and overnight hours, where the snow was mixed with sleet at times. On Friday, thunderstorms developed with dime sized hail being reported in Holt county.  The blizzard conditions continued Saturday morning, with conditions improving Saturday afternoon, as the snow and winds diminished. The heaviest snow occurred from the eastern panhandle into west central and north central Nebraska. These areas received 6 to 10 inches, with locally higher amounts of 12 to 14 inches. Strong northwest winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts from 50 to 60 mph at times, beginning Friday afternoon and continuing Friday night into Saturday morning. This created blizzard conditions and resulted in snow drifts several feet high and multiple highway closures, including Interstate 80.

A strong upper-level storm system tracked from the northern Rockies into northern High Plains with an associated surface low developing east across the Dakotas. Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening hours from north-central into eastern Nebraska along a trailing cold front with storms organizing into a bow-echo complex which produced numerous severe wind gusts and wind damage over northeast Nebraska, as well as a brief tornado near Madison, NE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs and power lines blown down in and around West Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers began moving east across Southwest Nebraska in the morning. There were several different waves of snow showers from the morning until after midnight. The repeated rounds of snow from these showers yielded snowfall amounts close to a foot across the east half of Dundy County into the west half of Hitchcock County. The highest snowfall amount of 12 was reported in Stratton. The band of higher snowfall amounts extended northeast into northwestern Red Willow County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall amount of 11 was reported at two different locations, a few miles north of Parks and a few miles north of Benkelman.  Snowfall amounts declined to the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system produced blizzard conditions across Southwest Nebraska.  The blizzard conditions caused highways to be closed.  The highest wind gust reported was 60 MPH at McCook.  The highest snowfall amount reported was 5 inches in Red Willow County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All highways across the county are closed due to whiteout conditions.  A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was reported at the McCook airport 10:37 PM CDT.  Snow amounts ranged from two to five inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow is not usually associated with Easter, but this Sunday looked and felt a lot more like winter than early spring across South Central Nebraska...albeit more so in some places than others. While most of the 24-county area received at least a dusting up to a few inches, a fairly narrow, roughly 15-25 mile wide corridor accumulated markedly higher amounts ranging generally 4-11. The heart of this band was oriented west-northwest to east-southeast, centered roughly along a line from Elwood-Minden-Blue Hill-Bruning. According to reports from a variety of official and unofficial sources, some of the highest totals concentrated near the Fillmore-Thayer County line, featuring: 11.0 at Carleton, 10.8 near Belvidere and 9.5 near Shickley. Totals from a few larger towns in and near this main band included: 8 in Minden and 6 in Holdrege, Blue Hill and Clay Center. Lower amounts within the northern and southern fringes of the band included: 5.1 at NWS Hastings, 3.8 at Kearney and 3.4 in Hebron. Despite fairly hefty snow amounts in some spots, this event was relatively low impact, with little-to-no blowing and drifting thanks to winds averaging 10 MPH-or-less. However, slick roads did contribute to a single-fatality traffic accident on the northeast side of Grand Island. ||As for timing, the majority of snow accumulated during the pre-dawn hours, starting in western counties around midnight and steadily spreading east within a few hours. After daybreak, mainly light snow hung on several more hours, ending in the west generally between 10 AM-noon CDT, and vacating far southeast areas around Hebron by 2 PM CDT. Turning to the meteorology behind this Easter event, this was a fairly textbook case of pronounced mid-level frontogenesis migrating across the Central Plains in broad west-northwest flow, especially evident around 700 millibars. At the surface, a sharp cold front had passed through on March 31st, ushering in an unseasonably-cold airmass. By the time snow broke out, a strong high pressure system (nearly 1030 millibars) settled into the region, keeping winds light. Although not record-breaking, high temperatures on the 1st averaged around 30 degrees below normal, with most of South Central Nebraska only topping out a few degrees either side of 30 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of 4.0 inches of snow was reported from Gothenburg. A cooperative observer measured a snowfall total of 3.3 inches in Cozad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow is not usually associated with Easter, but this Sunday looked and felt a lot more like winter than early spring across South Central Nebraska...albeit more so in some places than others. While most of the 24-county area received at least a dusting up to a few inches, a fairly narrow, roughly 15-25 mile wide corridor accumulated markedly higher amounts ranging generally 4-11. According to reports from a variety of official and unofficial sources, some of the highest totals concentrated near the Fillmore-Thayer County line, featuring: 11.0 at Carleton, 10.8 near Belvidere and 9.5 near Shickley. Lower amounts within the northern and southern fringes of the band included: 5.1 at NWS Hastings, 3.8 at Kearney and 3.4 in Hebron. Despite fairly hefty snow amounts in some spots, this event was relatively low impact, with little-to-no blowing and drifting thanks to winds averaging 10 MPH-or-less. However, slick roads did contribute to a single-fatality traffic accident on the northeast side of Grand Island. ||As for timing, the majority of snow accumulated during the pre-dawn hours, starting in western counties around midnight and steadily spreading east within a few hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sunday the 29th and Monday the 30th were two of the windiest consecutive days of spring 2018 within South Central Nebraska, especially in terms of SOUTH winds. On both days, primarily the afternoon featured several hours of sustained speeds commonly 25-35 MPH (isolated 40 MPH) and frequent gusts to around 50 MPH, along with isolated severe-criteria gusts of 58+ MPH. Also on both days, the combination of recent dry conditions and recently-tilled fields promoted a number of localized dust storms, causing drastic visibility reductions over short distances. Specifically on the late afternoon of the 29th, dust blowing across Interstate 80 near the Aurora exit led to a major pileup of 29 vehicles, closing I-80 for over two hours. Sadly, this incident resulted in one fatality and over a dozen injuries. ||As for measured winds from a variety of official and unofficial sources, a few peak gusts on the 29th featured: 62 MPH near Kearney (mesonet), 59 MPH near Oxford (mesonet), and 58 MPH at Grand Island and Ord airports. At Kearney Regional Airport, sustained winds averaged 40 MPH for an hour, mainly between 12-1 PM CDT. Although it remained quite breezy even after dark, gusts by late evening largely eased below 40 MPH. Turning to the meteorological background, this was a classic recipe for prolonged, strong south winds across the Central Plains. In the mid-upper levels, an expansive, large-scale trough gradually deepened over the western CONUS, only edging slowly eastward. This in turn promoted strengthening surface low pressure across the Central High Plains, averaging around 998 millibars in eastern Colorado. This low pressure, in concert with sprawling high pressure centered over the eastern CONUS, set up a healthy pressure gradient. During the height of the strongest winds on the 29th, the state of Nebraska featured roughly a 16-millibar gradient, ranging from around 1000 millibars in the western Panhandle, to around 1016 millibars in far southeast counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station just east-southeast of Kearney measured a 62 MPH gust.

Also on both days, the combination of recent dry conditions and recently-tilled fields promoted a number of localized dust storms, causing drastic visibility reductions over short distances. Specifically on the late afternoon of the 29th, dust blowing across Interstate 80 near the Aurora exit led to a major pileup of 29 vehicles, closing I-80 for over two hours. Sadly, this incident resulted in one fatality and over a dozen injuries. ||As for measured winds from a variety of official and unofficial sources, a few peak gusts on the 29th featured: 62 MPH near Kearney (mesonet), 59 MPH near Oxford (mesonet), and 58 MPH at Grand Island and Ord airports. At Kearney Regional Airport, sustained winds averaged 40 MPH for an hour, mainly between 12-1 PM CDT. Although it remained quite breezy even after dark, gusts by late evening largely eased below 40 MPH. Turning to the meteorological background, this was a classic recipe for prolonged, strong south winds across the Central Plains. In the mid-upper levels, an expansive, large-scale trough gradually deepened over the western CONUS, only edging slowly eastward. This in turn promoted strengthening surface low pressure across the Central High Plains, averaging around 998 millibars in eastern Colorado. This low pressure, in concert with sprawling high pressure centered over the eastern CONUS, set up a healthy pressure gradient. During the height of the strongest winds on the 29th, the state of Nebraska featured roughly a 16-millibar gradient, ranging from around 1000 millibars in the western Panhandle, to around 1016 millibars in far southeast counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the second of three peak gusts of 58 MPH at Central Nebraska Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a surface front over southern South Dakota during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 8th. The storms merged into a bow-echo complex which tracked southeast through eastern Nebraska during the early-morning hours of June 9th. The thunderstorms produced wind damage and a measured wind gust of 74 mph at the Lincoln Municipal Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person estimated 70 mph winds 5 miles south southeast of Creston.

A weak upper-level disturbance tracking through the central Plains triggered an early-day cluster of thunderstorms that intensified rapidly while moving through northeast Nebraska and west-central Iowa. Wind gusts well into the 70-mph range were recorded in Antelope, Madison, and Burt counties with the passage of the storms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large branches snapped off trees and corn flattened by damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe weather from Colorado spread north into Nebraska as the storms themselves moved eastward.  A peak gust of 71 MPH was reported at the McCook airport.  A large tree in Indianola was blown down from this same line of thunderstorm winds.

A strong winter storm system produced blizzard conditions across Southwest Nebraska.  The blizzard conditions caused highways to be closed.  The highest wind gust reported was 60 MPH at McCook.  The highest snowfall amount reported was 5 inches in Red Willow County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webcam at Haigler showed blizzard conditions.  One to four inches of snow was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in two different waves on a southwest to northeast orientated cold front across eastern Nebraska.  In the early morning of the 30th, a wave of thunderstorms moved northeast across northeastern Nebraska.  One of the storms intensified and became severe with large hail over Knox County.  Later in the afternoon,  thunderstorms redeveloped over southeast Nebraska and produced damaging wind and large hail, and between 2 to 3 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven miles north of Tobias, doors were ripped off of a shed, and another shed was destroyed with the tin thrown 1/4 mile away from the shed.  A tree was also split from damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system produced blizzard conditions across Southwest Nebraska.  The blizzard conditions caused highways to be closed.  The highest wind gust reported was 60 MPH at McCook.  The highest snowfall amount reported was 5 inches in Red Willow County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on blizzard reports from surrounding counties with the same bands that were over Hitchcock county, blizzard conditions should have occurred over the county.  Between one and four inches of snow likely fell across the county, based on snowfall reports in Dundy and Red Willow counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that produced severe weather across southeast Nebraska on April 13th, strengthened and produced snow across the area on the 13th and 15th.  Snowfall across Antelope, Pierce, Wayne, Cedar and Knox counties ranged from 4 to 8 inches.  In addition high winds caused blizzard conditions across parts of northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting up to 48 mph created visibilities of 1/4 mile  or less with blowing snow.  Total snowfall in Madison County ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance caused isolated severe thunderstorms to develop on  a weak frontal boundary over northeast Nebraska on the 23rd.  Thunderstorms produced large hail as big as tennis balls parts of northeast Nebraska in these storms.

During the afternoon a group of thunderstorms, behind a cold that was producing quarter size hail in Yuma County, extended east into Dundy County.  This part of the storm produced straight-line winds strong enough to bring down tree limbs up to eight inches in diameter near Haigler.   This group of storms transitioned to producing more straight-line winds than hail as they moved into Northwest Kansas.||In the early evening a separate round of storms moved south across Red Willow County.  These storms also caused wind damage, blowing numerous trees down onto Highway 89 between Danbury and Lebanon.  These storms continued to produce wind damage in Northwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs up to eight inches in diameter broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that had a history of producing nickel size hail in Yuma County moved through Dundy County behind a weak surface boundary.  Hail up to nickel size was reported in Haigler as the storm moved through.
A slow moving cold front coupled with decently strong zonal flow aloft brought a few severe thunderstorms to the area the first few days of July.
