Thunderstorms developed in two different waves on a southwest to northeast orientated cold front across eastern Nebraska.  In the early morning of the 30th, a wave of thunderstorms moved northeast across northeastern Nebraska.  One of the storms intensified and became severe with large hail over Knox County.  Later in the afternoon,  thunderstorms redeveloped over southeast Nebraska and produced brief tornadoes, damaging wind and large hail, and between 2 to 3 inches of rain. Another wave of storms late on the 30th, produced a brief tornado in Saline County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This brief, narrow,  tornado flattened corn, tore off roof panels of a shed, and collapsed some walls from a small shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in two different waves on a southwest to northeast orientated cold front across eastern Nebraska.  In the early morning of the 30th, a wave of thunderstorms moved northeast across northeastern Nebraska.  One of the storms intensified and became severe with large hail over Knox County.  Later in the afternoon,  thunderstorms redeveloped over southeast Nebraska and produced brief tornadoes, damaging wind and large hail, and between 2 to 3 inches of rain. Another wave of storms late on the 30th, produced a brief tornado in Saline County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado started approximately 7 3/4 miles south of Friend and traveled 1.4 miles  in a west to east direction.  A storage shed moved over 1000 feet from where it started.  Trunks of trees  and large tree limbs were snapped, and metal panels were blown off a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance tracking through the central Plains triggered an early-day cluster of thunderstorms that intensified rapidly while moving through northeast Nebraska and west-central Iowa. Wind gusts well into the 70-mph range were recorded in Antelope, Madison, and Burt counties with the passage of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 79 mph was measured 1 mile north northwest of Newman Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance tracked from the central High Plains through the mid Missouri Valley, encouraging the development of a surface low from southeast South Dakota into eastern Nebraska.  Meanwhile, an associated cold front progressed east into eastern Nebraska by afternoon with a stationary boundary extending from the surface low through southwest Iowa into northern Missouri. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa along these surface features with activity merging into a bow-echo complex that subsequently tracked into northwest Missouri by late evening. Brief tornadoes occurred  with a supercell over Cass County, NE with intense, straight-line winds affecting the I-29 corridor in Fremont County, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was flooding northeast of Cedar Creek .  Five feet of water was reported in a garage as well as flooding at the local baseball fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary surface front from central Nebraska to central Iowa served as the focus for storm development during the late afternoon and evening hours. A hot and humid air mass to the south of the boundary fueled widely scattered severe storms which produced hail to tennis-ball size, damaging winds, and localized flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in two different waves on a southwest to northeast orientated cold front across eastern Nebraska.  In the early morning of the 30th, a wave of thunderstorms moved northeast across northeastern Nebraska.  One of the storms intensified and became severe with large hail over Knox County.  Later in the afternoon,  thunderstorms redeveloped over southeast Nebraska and produced brief tornadoes, damaging wind and large hail, and between 2 to 3 inches of rain. Another wave of storms late on the 30th, produced a brief tornado in Saline County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance advanced from the central High Plains into mid Missouri Valley, supporting the development of a surface low over central Nebraska during the afternoon. Severe thunderstorms developed by mid afternoon ahead of the surface low across eastern Nebraska with activity spreading east into western Iowa by evening.  The storms produced damaging straight-line winds with a gust to 76 mph observed near Pilger in Stanton County. A few brief tornadoes were also reported in Stanton, Burt, and Mills Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser measured a 70 mph wind gust 4 miles west of Bancroft.

As water drained into the headwaters of the Little Blue River, the river rapidly rose with minor flooding occurring. At Deweese, the river gauge peaked at 10.86 ft during the day Monday. Flood Stage is 10.0 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across the area, ranging anywhere from 3 to 6 inches, resulted in a few rural roads being washed out in the Holstein area. Flooding was also reported in the Crystal Lake State Recreation Area, and while the park was not closed, campers were encouraged to leave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred between Red Cloud and Hebron late in the afternoon on this Thursday. Throughout the morning and early afternoon, a narrow band of light rain, oriented from southwest-northeast, moved from the Nebraska panhandle eastward. By mid-afternoon, this rain band moved into south central Nebraska. About 60 miles ahead of this rain band, scattered thunderstorms began forming, where skies were sunny, from Red Cloud to York. These storms were part of a much larger line that extended from Souix Falls, South Dakota to Hill City, Kansas. Thunderstorms continued to increase in coverage, filling-in between Red Cloud and York. Between 4 and 6 PM CST, some storms became severe. While the majority of storms were still predominantly individual cells, hail occurred in a couple spots over Clay and Webster counties. The largest hail reported was the size of ping pong balls and golf balls in the town of Cowles. Between 5 and 6 PM CST, a squall line formed from Highway 136 in Webster county down into north central Kansas. The northern portion of this line became a small-scale bow echo and moved northeast across Nuckolls, Thayer, and Fillmore counties. Damaging downburst winds snapped off large tree limbs in Red Cloud,  Hebron, and Bruning. Winds were measured at 64 MPH in Nelson and 65 MPH in Davenport.||In the preceding days, an east-west oriented front had become quasi-stationary from the Ohio Valley across central Nebraska to Nevada. During the morning hours on this date, a low formed on the front between North Platte and McCook. This low then accelerated to near Sioux City, Iowa by mid-afternoon with its trailing cold front becoming mobile and moving southeast. The storms formed along this front. In the upper-levels, a subtropical high was over the Tennessee Valley and gradually moved into the Carolinas as a fairly strong shortwave trough moved out of the Northern Rockies into Northern and Central Plains. Immediately ahead of the front, temperatures were in the lower to middle 90s with dewpoints in the lower to middle 60s. Combined with mid-level lapse rates between 7.0 and 7.5 C/km, SBCAPE was between 1500 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was near 40 kt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large tree limbs were downed by wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance tracked from the central High Plains through the mid Missouri Valley, encouraging the development of a surface low from southeast South Dakota into eastern Nebraska.  Meanwhile, an associated cold front progressed east into eastern Nebraska by afternoon with a stationary boundary extending from the surface low through southwest Iowa into northern Missouri. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa along these surface features with activity merging into a bow-echo complex that subsequently tracked into northwest Missouri by late evening. Brief tornadoes occurred  with a supercell over Cass County, NE with intense, straight-line winds affecting the I-29 corridor in Fremont County, IA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Friday morning-early afternoon featured several hours of isolated to scattered thunderstorm activity rumbling across South Central Nebraska from west-to-east, starting as early as 6-8 a.m. CDT in far western counties and departing eastern areas between 11 a.m. and 1 p.m. CDT. While the vast majority of this elevated convection was quite weak, one storm briefly pulsed up to severe levels over the Kearney-Phelps County line around 9 a.m. CDT, yielding a report of quarter size hail in Axtell. ||In the mid-upper levels, this marginally-severe storm occurred within a fairly subtly-forced regime, with the local area seeing only passing weak ripples embedded within broad west-southwest flow. At the surface, a weak warm front stretched west-to-east along the Nebraska-Kansas border, attendant to a low pressure center in northwest KS. Despite modest elevated instability and weak deep-layer wind shear, this storm sufficiently tapped into a reservoir of steep mid-level lapse rates to briefly go severe.

A strong upper-level wave and jet entrance region pushed into the Northern Plains, with a tight mid-level frontal boundary stalling across the area for several hours within confluent southwest flow aloft. Unseasonably cold air rushed into the region behind a southward surging low-level frontal boundary, with developing precipitation making a very rapid transition from rain to snow. Snow accumulated quickly in the overnight and early morning hours due to proximity of slightly unstable air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snow accumulated to 2 to 4 inches across portions of northeast Nebraska. Snowfall of 2.6 inches was measured at Newcastle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level wave and frontal boundary pushed across the area during the afternoon hours.  Storms erupted as temperatures cooled aloft by early afternoon, tapping instability of 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE.  Deep-layer shear over 50 knots was primarily unidirectional, but storms maintained largely isolated nature along the boundary, producing periodic large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this Wednesday morning technically featured no severe-criteria hail/wind reports, a southward-drifting cluster of strong storms still managed to create a bit of commotion. Most notably, around 7:45 a.m. CDT, a suspected lightning strike ignited a fire at a small oil storage tank facility near the Franklin-Harlan County line north of Naponee. Four of the five tanks were destroyed in the fire, which produced a plume of dark black smoke that could be seen for miles. Prior to sparking the fire, this storm cluster yielded a slightly sub-severe wind gust of 55 MPH at the Holdrege airport and a report of penny size hail in Wilcox. ||Timing-wise, this convective cluster first entered South Central Nebraska between 4:30-5:30 a.m. CDT, dropping southward into eastern Dawson/western Buffalo counties. Over the next few hours, storms continued nearly due-south before exiting into north central Kansas between 8-9 a.m. CDT. In the mid-upper levels, this activity was sparked by a series of weak disturbances passing southward through the region in northwesterly flow, directed between a large-scale trough centered over eastern Canada/the Great Lakes, and a trough over the southwestern United States. Mesoscale convective parameters over the local area featured around 1500 J/kg of most-unstable CAPE, but no more than 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

Late on this Friday evening the 31st into the very early morning hours of Saturday, Sep. 1, a compact cluster of strong to severe storms rolled through the heart of South Central Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast, resulting in several reports of mainly damaging winds, especially in the Hastings, Grand Island and Central City areas. PLEASE NOTE: The majority of severe weather occurred after midnight CST Sep. 1. As a result, several more storm reports and details can be found within September 2018 Storm Data for South Central Nebraska. ||Focusing hereafter only on the late evening of Aug. 31, the lone report of severe weather prior to midnight CST consisted of hail up to quarter size in far northeastern Furnas County (shortly after 11:30 p.m. CST). This hail occurred early-on in the evolution of the severe storm cluster, which was in the process of rapidly intensifying as several weaker storms merged. This intensification took place 1-2 hours after the leading edge of initially-disorganized convection entered the far western counties of South Central Nebraska between 10-11 p.m. CST. These storms formed along and ahead of a Canadian cold front. The flow aloft was west-southwest from California to Maine and low in amplitude. There was no strong forcing aloft affecting the Central Plains. Before the thunderstorms moved in, temperatures were in the middle 70s with dewpoints in the lower 70s. Mid-level lapse rates were very steep, near 8.5 C/km. This resulted in very high instability with MUCAPE of 3000-4000 J/kg. However, deep layer shear was modest, only around 30 knots.

Considering that intense/damaging storms are fairly rare in South Central Nebraska during the pre-dawn hours, what happened in Howard County on this Thursday morning was rather noteworthy. During a 20-30 minute period centered either side of 4 a.m. CDT, a brief-but-powerful severe storm swept from northwest-to-southeast across mainly the southern half of the county, yielding a nasty combination of copious amounts of hail (mostly at-or-below quarter size, but up to golf ball size) and damaging winds estimated up to 75 MPH. In a few spots, hail drifts were reportedly ankle to knee high. Needless to say, significant crop damage occurred from around Farwell, southeastward through areas between St. Paul and Dannebrog, and on toward St. Libory. One spotter reported at least 1000 acres destroyed just in their local area, with corn shredded to knee-high. In addition to crop destruction, several irrigation pivots were flipped over, large tree limbs downed, and grain bins damaged, including one near Farwell that was blown off its foundation. Furthermore, emergency management reported at least 12 power poles damaged or snapped, and at a residence two miles east-southeast of Farwell, a carport was destroyed and a barn door was ripped off (in addition to tree damage).||This particular storm was far from the only one to affect mainly northern portions of South Central Nebraska during the late night/early morning hours, as several others roamed areas north of Interstate 80 between midnight and sunrise, most remaining innocuous . However, this storm (for whatever reason) just happened to reach severe levels. It first started showing signs of becoming a problem over the Valley-Sherman County line around 3:30 a.m. CDT. During the ensuing hour, it rapidly dove southeastward, causing the aforementioned damage in Howard County as radar signatures morphed into a mini-bow echo. However, as quickly as this storm intensified, it weakened almost as abruptly, dropping below severe limits by the time it crossed into southwestern Merrick and far northeastern Hall counties by 4:30 a.m. CDT. In the mid-upper levels, forcing was evident, as the local area resided along the southern periphery of a compact/vigorous low pressure system churning along the Nebraska-South Dakota border. While the intensity of this storm was a bit of a surprise given the typically-unfavorable time of day (especially for damaging winds), the seasonably-strong upper forcing, in tandem with around 1000 J/kg of most-unstable CAPE and at least 30-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear, proved sufficient to yield such a surprise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of hail up to the size of golf balls and wind gusts of 60-75 MPH resulted in damage across central/southern Howard County. Most of the hail reports were dime to quarter size, with one report of golf ball hail north of Dannebrog, but a common theme was the copious amounts of hail. In a few spots, hail drifts were ankle-knee high. There were many reports of significant crop damage extending from around Farwell southeastward into the St. Paul and St. Libory areas. One spotter reported at least 1000 acres were destroyed just in their local area, with corn knocked down to just knee-high. Several irrigation pivots were tipped over, large tree limbs downed, and grain bins damaged, including one east-southeast of Farwell that was blown off its foundation. Emergency management reported that at least 12 power poles were damaged or snapped. At a residence 2 miles east-southeast of Farwell, a carport was destroyed and a barn door was ripped off (in addition to tree damage).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Monday evening got somewhat more active than anticipated, as a pair of supercell storms affected mainly the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska, resulting in several reports of large hail (up to 2 diameter), damaging winds (up to around 75 MPH), and even one EF-1 tornado. This tornado (spawned by the eastern supercell) touched down in northwestern York County around 7:45 p.m. CDT and tracked east-southeast for less than 2 miles, causing minor damage to a home while also damaging several grain bins and snapping a few large trees. The largest hail reports (2) originated from the Bradshaw and Alda areas. In addition, golf ball size hail occurred near Benedict and Waco, and much of the city of York saw a few minutes of hail to at least half dollar size. As for thunderstorm winds, the highest measured gust was 60 MPH at the York airport. However, especially the eastern supercell produced a concentrated swath of damaging winds, estimated to around 75 MPH in some places. Crop damage (from a combo of both hail/wind) was extensive along this storm's track from central Merrick County into northeastern Hamilton County and northwestern-through-east-central York County. ||Taking a closer look at event evolution/timing, a smattering of weak, elevated storms got underway as early as early-mid afternoon across various parts of the 24-county area, producing little more than brief downpours and small hail. By late afternoon, another round of fairly weak storms flared up within counties mainly north of Interstate 80. However, between 6:30-8:30 p.m. CDT, the situation escalated as one of these initially-weak storms rapidly became severe over Howard County, becoming a legitimate supercell as it tracked east-southeast through parts of Merrick-northeastern Hamilton and York counties. This storm yielded the majority of local severe weather reports/damage (including the York County tornado), before exiting into eastern Nebraska shortly after 8:30 p.m. CDT. Meanwhile, back to the west, another supercell initiated over far northeastern Buffalo County around 8 p.m. CDT. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Monday evening got somewhat more active than anticipated, as a pair of supercell storms affected mainly the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska, resulting in several reports of large hail (up to 2 diameter), damaging winds (up to around 75 MPH), and even one EF-1 tornado. This tornado (spawned by the eastern supercell) touched down in northwestern York County around 7:45 p.m. CDT and tracked east-southeast for less than 2 miles, causing minor damage to a home while also damaging several grain bins and snapping a few large trees. The largest hail reports (2) originated from the Bradshaw and Alda areas. In addition, golf ball size hail occurred near Benedict and Waco, and much of the city of York saw a few minutes of hail to at least half dollar size. As for thunderstorm winds, the highest measured gust was 60 MPH at the York airport. However, especially the eastern supercell produced a concentrated swath of damaging winds, estimated to around 75 MPH in some places. Crop damage (from a combo of both hail/wind) was extensive along this storm's track from central Merrick County into northeastern Hamilton County and northwestern-through-east-central York County. ||Taking a closer look at event evolution/timing, a smattering of weak, elevated storms got underway as early as early-mid afternoon across various parts of the 24-county area, producing little more than brief downpours and small hail. By late afternoon, another round of fairly weak storms flared up within counties mainly north of Interstate 80. However, between 6:30-8:30 p.m. CDT, the situation escalated as one of these initially-weak storms rapidly became severe over Howard County, becoming a legitimate supercell as it tracked east-southeast through parts of Merrick-northeastern Hamilton and York counties. This storm yielded the majority of local severe weather reports/damage (including the York County tornado), before exiting into eastern Nebraska shortly after 8:30 p.m. CDT. Meanwhile, back to the west, another supercell initiated over far northeastern Buffalo County around 8 p.m. CDT. Like its predecessor, it also tracked east-southeast for a few hours,  producing large hail in the Alda and Stockham areas before weakening over northern Fillmore County around 10 p.m. CDT. Thereafter, non-severe storms continued percolating well into the night within areas mainly south of Interstate 80 and east of Highway 281, with all of South Central Nebraska finally storm-free by 3 a.m. CDT on the 7th. ||In hindsight, this was actually a decent setup for supercell storm development. However, right up until the early evening hours, locations as far north as Merrick/York counties seemed somewhat unfavorable for higher-end severe potential, especially given that the primary west-east oriented surface front resided well to the south over northern Kansas. However, even in the post-frontal airmass over South Central Nebraska, the combo of afternoon high temperatures into the 80s F and dewpoints in the upper 60s-low 70s F promoted mixed-layer CAPE around 2000 J/kg. This healthy instability, in tandem with seasonably-strong deep-layer wind shear of 40-50 knots and modest forcing provided by a shortwave trough invading from the northwest, proved plenty-sufficient to support a few supercells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 2 inches in diameter was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This brief EF-1 tornado occurred in northwest York County, causing damage approximately 7 miles north of Bradshaw. A home was damaged, mainly to soffits and garage doors, and there was also some roof damage. Grain bins were damaged along with several trees. Peak winds were estimated to be 90 MPH.||This tornado was associated with a supercell and a much larger scale wind and hail event in the region. There were numerous irrigation pivots overturned or damaged, and crop damage was widespread, especially around and just to the east of the tornado path. Some soybean fields were stripped and beat to the ground, and corn fields were cut down by the wind and hail to only three feet tall in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this Wednesday morning technically featured no severe-criteria hail/wind reports, a southward-drifting cluster of strong storms still managed to create a bit of commotion. Most notably, around 7:45 a.m. CDT, a suspected lightning strike ignited a fire at a small oil storage tank facility near the Franklin-Harlan County line north of Naponee. Four of the five tanks were destroyed in the fire, which produced a plume of dark black smoke that could be seen for miles. Prior to sparking the fire, this storm cluster yielded a slightly sub-severe wind gust of 55 MPH at the Holdrege airport and a report of penny size hail in Wilcox. ||Timing-wise, this convective cluster first entered South Central Nebraska between 4:30-5:30 a.m. CDT, dropping southward into eastern Dawson/western Buffalo counties. Over the next few hours, storms continued nearly due-south before exiting into north central Kansas between 8-9 a.m. CDT. In the mid-upper levels, this activity was sparked by a series of weak disturbances passing southward through the region in northwesterly flow, directed between a large-scale trough centered over eastern Canada/the Great Lakes, and a trough over the southwestern United States. Mesoscale convective parameters over the local area featured around 1500 J/kg of most-unstable CAPE, but no more than 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a lightning strike to an area of oil storage tanks, resulting in a fire. This fire destroyed 4 of the 5 storage tanks located at the facility. The time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure forced a cold front through western and north central Nebraska on June 1st. Along and ahead of the front, thunderstorms initiated over northeastern Cherry county. As storms tracked east, they became better organized and extended from Boyd county, south to Custer county during the evening hours. Activity exited western and north central Nebraska by 11 PM CDT. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were reported.

Along and ahead of the front, thunderstorms initiated over northeastern Cherry county. As storms tracked east, they became better organized and extended from Boyd county, south to Custer county during the evening hours. Activity exited western and north central Nebraska by 11 PM CDT. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were reported.

The severe weather from Colorado spread north into Nebraska as the storms themselves moved eastward.  A peak gust of 71 MPH was reported at the McCook airport.  A large tree in Indianola was blown down from this same line of thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was destroyed and a camper trailer was moved at a residence in Indianola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over far northeastern Colorado mid afternoon on June 17th and tracked into Deuel where they became severe. Storms moved east northeast into the Sandhills and remained severe through the mid evening hours. Large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes were reported from Deuel county, east northeast into Blaine county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged in size from nickles to golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of Alberta clipper systems moving across the plains left behind a swath of light to moderate snow accumulations across northeast Nebraska. Much of the area received from 3 to 5 inches snowfall, while wind chills dropped below zero by the end of the time of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, producing hazardous travel conditions with snowfall totaling as much as 4.3 inches at Newcastle and 1.5 inches at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced widespread high winds between Interstate 80 and Highway 6 on this Thursday night and Friday morning. During the mid to late-afternoon hours Thursday, scattered thunderstorms developed from the Nebraska Panhandle down into northeast Colorado. Storm mergers, and an increasing number of storms, resulted in the formation of a squall line by 700 PM CST. This line of storms crossed most of south central Nebraska between 10 PM CST on the 10th and 1 AM CST on the 11th. These storms produced widespread measured gusts between 58 and 70 mph along the entire way across south central Nebraska from the Interstate 80 corridor south to Highway 6. A couple unofficial weather stations measured higher wind speeds including 80 mph near Axtell and 78 near Norman, both in Kearney County. Despite the coverage and magnitude of the winds, damage was only reported in a few spots. Tree branches were snapped off in Minden, an irrigation pivot was overturned near Glenvil, a few trees were blown down in Central City, and six utility poles were blown down or snapped off near Harvard. There were reports in southern Adams County and western Clay County of zero visibility in blowing dust as the high winds from the outflow moved in before the rain fell. Farmers had been preparing the fields for planting. So, top soil was loose and available for easy lofting and transport. Behind the squall line, other scattered thunderstorms developed. The strongest storms moved east along the entire length of Interstate 80 from Lexington to York between 1230 AM and 330 AM CST. These storms were mostly sub-severe, but there was a single report of one inch hail in Kearney at 2 AM CST.||During the morning hours of Thursday, a strong southward-moving cold front crossed Nebraska and became stationary along the northern Kansas border. A low pressure center formed over Wyoming and, by early evening, began moving east along the front. Meanwhile, chilly high pressure moved from the Canadian Prairies into the Great Lakes region. A broad upper-level ridge was over the Plains with a trough along the East Coast and another trough advancing through the western states. MUCAPE was around 1500 J/kg immediately preceding the storms. These storms formed on the northeast fringe of an elevated mixed layer that was advecting into the region, ahead of the western trough. Deep layer shear was 50 kts and greater. This environment was more than sufficient for severe thunderstorms. The cold pool associated with the squall line was strong. The temperature at Lexington dropped 16 deg F in 20 minutes (76 to 60 deg F) as the gust front moved through, and 23 deg in 40 min (76 to 53 deg F).

Despite the coverage and magnitude of the winds, damage was only reported in a few spots. Tree branches were snapped off in Minden, an irrigation pivot was overturned near Glenvil, a few trees were blown down in Central City, and six utility poles were blown down or snapped off near Harvard. There were reports in southern Adams County and western Clay County of zero visibility in blowing dust as the high winds from the outflow moved in before the rain fell. Farmers had been preparing the fields for planting. So, top soil was loose and available for easy lofting and transport. Behind the squall line, other scattered thunderstorms developed. The strongest storms moved east along the entire length of Interstate 80 from Lexington to York between 1230 AM and 330 AM CST. These storms were mostly sub-severe, but there was a single report of one inch hail in Kearney at 2 AM CST.||During the morning hours of Thursday, a strong southward-moving cold front crossed Nebraska and became stationary along the northern Kansas border. A low pressure center formed over Wyoming and, by early evening, began moving east along the front. Meanwhile, chilly high pressure moved from the Canadian Prairies into the Great Lakes region. A broad upper-level ridge was over the Plains with a trough along the East Coast and another trough advancing through the western states. MUCAPE was around 1500 J/kg immediately preceding the storms. These storms formed on the northeast fringe of an elevated mixed layer that was advecting into the region, ahead of the western trough. Deep layer shear was 50 kts and greater. This environment was more than sufficient for severe thunderstorms. The cold pool associated with the squall line was strong. The temperature at Lexington dropped 16 deg F in 20 minutes (76 to 60 deg F) as the gust front moved through, and 23 deg in 40 min (76 to 53 deg F).

A major winter storm moved into the Central Plains on the 22nd.  A band of snow dumped over 10 inches of snow over a large area of northeast Nebraska.  Amounts of snow ranged from 1.8 inches in Lincoln to 17.6 inches in Bloomfield.  The largest amounts included 10 inches in Albion, 12.5 inches near Wakefield, 14 inches at Neligh, 14.6 inches at Norfolk, 15 inches at Stanton, and 16 inches at Pierce.  The heavy snow combined with strong winds created blizzard conditions across a large area of Northeastern Nebraska.  Roads were closed, and traffic came to a standstill on many highways.  Numerous power outages were reported as far south as Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense winds along with heavy snow created blizzard conditions across Pierce County.  There was a total of 16 of snow reported at Pierce.

The temperature at Lexington dropped 16 deg F in 20 minutes (76 to 60 deg F) as the gust front moved through, and 23 deg in 40 min (76 to 53 deg F).

Severe thunderstorms produced widespread high winds between Interstate 80 and Highway 6 on this Thursday night and Friday morning. During the mid to late-afternoon hours Thursday, scattered thunderstorms developed from the Nebraska Panhandle down into northeast Colorado. Storm mergers, and an increasing number of storms, resulted in the formation of a squall line by 700 PM CST. This line of storms crossed most of south central Nebraska between 10 PM CST on the 10th and 1 AM CST on the 11th. These storms produced widespread measured gusts between 58 and 70 mph along the entire way across south central Nebraska from the Interstate 80 corridor south to Highway 6. A couple unofficial weather stations measured higher wind speeds including 80 mph near Axtell and 78 near Norman, both in Kearney County. Despite the coverage and magnitude of the winds, damage was only reported in a few spots. Tree branches were snapped off in Minden, an irrigation pivot was overturned near Glenvil, a few trees were blown down in Central City, and six utility poles were blown down or snapped off near Harvard. There were reports in southern Adams County and western Clay County of zero visibility in blowing dust as the high winds from the outflow moved in before the rain fell. Farmers had been preparing the fields for planting. So, top soil was loose and available for easy lofting and transport. Behind the squall line, other scattered thunderstorms developed. The strongest storms moved east along the entire length of Interstate 80 from Lexington to York between 1230 AM and 330 AM CST. These storms were mostly sub-severe, but there was a single report of one inch hail in Kearney at 2 AM CST.||During the morning hours of Thursday, a strong southward-moving cold front crossed Nebraska and became stationary along the northern Kansas border. A low pressure center formed over Wyoming and, by early evening, began moving east along the front. Meanwhile, chilly high pressure moved from the Canadian Prairies into the Great Lakes region. A broad upper-level ridge was over the Plains with a trough along the East Coast and another trough advancing through the western states. MUCAPE was around 1500 J/kg immediately preceding the storms. These storms formed on the northeast fringe of an elevated mixed layer that was advecting into the region, ahead of the western trough. Deep layer shear was 50 kts and greater. This environment was more than sufficient for severe thunderstorms. The cold pool associated with the squall line was strong. The temperature at Lexington dropped 16 deg F in 20 minutes (76 to 60 deg F) as the gust front moved through, and 23 deg in 40 min (76 to 53 deg F).

Spotty wind damage occurred in Cozad after midnight on this Friday morning, followed by severe winds associated with a wake low later in the morning. At 1200 AM CST, a large mesoscale convective system was in progress from the Sandhills southwest into the southern Nebraska Panhandle. The forward-propagating squall line extended from the Sandhills southwest into Custer County and was weakening. Where the outflow boundary became oriented more west-east, a few strong storms remained. These storms formed into a new squall line over Lincoln and Custer counties. As this line moved into Dawson County, some minor roof damage occurred in Cozad and a large pine tree was blown over and onto a house. This line weakened as it moved further east into south central Nebraska. After sunrise, the persistent mesoscale convective complex that had affected the area, throughout the nighttime and early morning hours, weakened and decreased in size. As this occurred, a wake low developed on its westward side and resulted in measured winds of 60 mph at the airport in Ord, along with significant fluctuations in pressure.||Two fronts separated three air masses over the U.S., one across the southern states and the other across the northern states. A chilly continental polar air mass was across the north, a warm and humid maritime tropical air mass was over the south, and these storms occurred in the modified continental polar air in between, far from any fronts. An upper-level trough was over the western U.S., while a ridge was over the Great Lakes. Fairly weak southwest flow was over the Central Plains. This MCS developed from scattered storms that initially formed within the lee trough near the Nebraska-Wyoming border the previous afternoon. Warm air advection and convergence associated with the low-level jet maintained the MCS through the night. Just prior to the squall line affecting Cozad, MUCAPE was around 1500 J/kg with mid-level lapse rates around 7.5 deg C/km. Deep layer shear was around 45 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few local businesses sustained minor roof damage, and some tin was blown off a few outbuildings.  A large pine tree was uprooted, landing on a house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This wind gust was associated with a wake low. The pressure plunged 9 mb between 9-10 a.m. CDT. There was a 3 mb pressure drop from 9:55-10 a.m. CDT and then a 3 mb rise from 10-10:05 a.m. CDT. This gust coincided with the lowest pressure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms produced numerous reports of hail and 3 brief, weak tornadoes on this Tuesday afternoon and evening. Between 230 and 330 PM CST, scattered fairly small supercell thunderstorms developed along a line from Red Cloud to Grand Island to Columbus. They were not mini-supercells, though, as cloud tops were near 40,000 ft. The storms generally remained along that line except for one storm that moved east into Clay County and another small multicell cluster that moved east along the Nebraska-Kansas border. Nearly 30 occurrences of severe hail were reported with most of between the sizes of quarters and golf balls. The largest hail reported was 2 inches in diameter in the town of Roseland. Three brief EF-0 tornadoes also occurred. The storms from Grand Island to Columbus formed along a slow-moving cold front. One tornado was associated with a supercell located on or very close to the front just west of Aurora. Another tornado formed near Doniphan as a north-northeastward-moving storm crossed the front. The third tornado occurred with the storm that moved east along the Nebraska-Kansas border. This storm evolved from a small bow echo and exhibited a spiral shape as it produced a brief tornado near Chester. The storms dissipated or moved out of south central Nebraska by 6 PM CST.||A low pressure system was near Lake Superior with a slow-moving cold front extending southwest across Nebraska to the Oklahoma Panhandle. In the upper-levels, southwest flow covered most of the U.S., with a trough over the West and a ridge over the Northeast. Just prior to thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the lower 80s with dewpoints in the upper 50s to near 60. With mid-level lapse rates near 8 C/km, MLCAPE was 2000-2500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 40-50 kts, which was more than sufficient for supercell convective mode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-0 rated tornado moved in from Republic County, Kansas. The tornado was captured on video by a local farmer. The video and radar was used to determine the time. This tornado did not cause any significant damage as it moved through an open field, and the maximum wind speed was estimated to be 70 MPH.

Isolated severe winds occurred in Buffalo County on this Monday, along with locally heavy rain. Thunderstorms were widespread over southwest Nebraska and the western fringe of south central Nebraska (west of Hwy 283) at sunrise. This area of storms decreased and became more scattered as it advanced further east into south central Nebraska late in the morning. New thunderstorms formed over southwestern Buffalo County around 1030 AM CST. As they moved northeast across the county, one observer north of Kearney estimated winds may have been as high as 60 mph. There was no nearby measurements or reports of damage to substantiate the observation, though. Multiple storms occurred over southwest Hall County between 1200 and 100 PM CST, and lighter anvil-generated rain continued thereafter. An observer measured just over 3 inches of rain.||Scattered thunderstorms originally formed far to the west along and ahead of a cold front the previous afternoon. The front remained quasi-stationary across northern and western Nebraska during the day Monday. An outflow boundary from the overnight MCS did move southeast across the state, aiding in the initiation of additional thunderstorms. A persistent trough was over the Western U.S. with an embedded cut-off low, with a downstream ridge over the Upper Midwest. Fairly weak southwest flow was over the Central Plains. In advance of the storms, temperatures had risen into the 80s with dewpoints in the 60s. Combined with mid-level lapse rates near 7 C/km, this resulted in MLCAPE between 1500 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 25-30 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving southward across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa caused some flash freezing conditions on wet roadway surfaces.  In addition an area of snow developed behind the front.  Amounts between 3 to 5 inches fell over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 3.7 inches of snow was reported 5 miles east of Auburn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved into the Central Plains on the 22nd.  A band of snow dumped over 10 inches of snow over a large area of northeast Nebraska.  Amounts of snow ranged from 1.8 inches in Lincoln to 17.6 inches in Bloomfield.  The largest amounts included 10 inches in Albion, 12.5 inches near Wakefield, 14 inches at Neligh, 14.6 inches at Norfolk, 15 inches at Stanton, and 16 inches at Pierce.  The heavy snow combined with strong winds created blizzard conditions across a large area of Northeastern Nebraska.  Roads were closed, and traffic came to a standstill on many highways.  Numerous power outages were reported as far south as Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds and heavy snowfall occurred across much of Butler County.  Whiteout conditions and zero visibility was experienced by motorists. Snowfall across the county ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple thunderstorms briefly became severe on this Tuesday. Scattered showers and thunderstorms occurred throughout the day. During the late afternoon and early evening hours, a couple storms produced low-end severe weather, with a single report of 3/4 inch hail just south of the airport in Hastings. A separate storm may have produced severe winds in southern Thayer County where a spotter estimated winds around 60 mph near the town of Hubbell. However, three other spotters nearby estimated winds at 50 mph. Severe thunderstorms produced widespread high winds between Interstate 80 and Highway 6 on this Thursday night and Friday morning. During the mid to late-afternoon hours Thursday, scattered thunderstorms developed from the Nebraska Panhandle down into northeast Colorado. Storm mergers, and an increasing number of storms, resulted in the formation of a squall line by 700 PM CST. This line of storms crossed most of south central Nebraska between 10 PM CST on the 10th and 1 AM CST on the 11th. These storms produced widespread measured gusts between 58 and 70 mph along the entire way across south central Nebraska from the Interstate 80 corridor south to Highway 6. A couple unofficial weather stations measured higher wind speeds including 80 mph near Axtell and 78 near Norman, both in Kearney County. Despite the coverage and magnitude of the winds, damage was only reported in a few spots. Tree branches were snapped off in Minden, an irrigation pivot was overturned near Glenvil, a few trees were blown down in Central City, and six utility poles were blown down or snapped off near Harvard. There were reports in southern Adams County and western Clay County of zero visibility in blowing dust as the high winds from the outflow moved in before the rain fell. Farmers had been preparing the fields for planting. So, top soil was loose and available for easy lofting and transport. Behind the squall line, other scattered thunderstorms developed. The strongest storms moved east along the entire length of Interstate 80 from Lexington to York between 1230 AM and 330 AM CST. These storms were mostly sub-severe, but there was a single report of one inch hail in Kearney at 2 AM CST.||During the morning hours of Thursday, a strong southward-moving cold front crossed Nebraska and became stationary along the northern Kansas border. A low pressure center formed over Wyoming and, by early evening, began moving east along the front. Meanwhile, chilly high pressure moved from the Canadian Prairies into the Great Lakes region. A broad upper-level ridge was over the Plains with a trough along the East Coast and another trough advancing through the western states. MUCAPE was around 1500 J/kg immediately preceding the storms. These storms formed on the northeast fringe of an elevated mixed layer that was advecting into the region, ahead of the western trough. Deep layer shear was 50 kts and greater. This environment was more than sufficient for severe thunderstorms. The cold pool associated with the squall line was strong. The temperature at Lexington dropped 16 deg F in 20 minutes (76 to 60 deg F) as the gust front moved through, and 23 deg in 40 min (76 to 53 deg F).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 65 MPH resulted in multiple downed tree branches in Minden.

A major winter storm moved into the Central Plains on the 22nd.  A band of snow dumped over 10 inches of snow over a large area of northeast Nebraska.  Amounts of snow ranged from 1.8 inches in Lincoln to 17.6 inches in Bloomfield.  The largest amounts included 10 inches in Albion, 12.5 inches near Wakefield, 14 inches at Neligh, 14.6 inches at Norfolk, 15 inches at Stanton, and 16 inches at Pierce.  The heavy snow combined with strong winds created blizzard conditions across a large area of Northeastern Nebraska.  Roads were closed, and traffic came to a standstill on many highways.  Numerous power outages were reported as far south as Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions were reported across the county.  Snowfall in Seward was around 3 inches. There was an accident on Interstate 80 where 14 vehicles piled up in Seward County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense winds combined with a snow total of 15 produced blizzard conditions across Stanton County.  U.S. Highway 275 was closed between Nebraska Highway 15 and 57 due to several wrecks and jackknifed semi trucks.  Sixteen people had to be rescued from their vehicles on U.S. 275 in Stanton County.  There were some snow drifts as high as 7 feet tall on east-west roads in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving southward across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa caused some flash freezing conditions on wet roadway surfaces.  In addition an area of snow developed behind the front.  Amounts between 3 to 5 inches fell over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.5 inches of snow was reported at Murray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced damaging winds in Richardson County which blew down a tree and caused the power to be lost to all of the residents of Rulo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in Rulo from thunderstorm winds.  All of Rulo was without power for about one hour due to the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow fell across much of the Nebraska Panhandle producing in some locations 6 to 8 inches of snow. Heaviest snowfall amounts  reported in Sioux, Morrill and Cheyenne Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located one mile west-northwest of Gurley measured 6.0 inches of heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow fell across much of the Nebraska Panhandle producing in some locations 6 to 8 inches of snow. Heaviest snowfall amounts  reported in Sioux, Morrill and Cheyenne Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer in Chadron reported 6.0 inches of heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms developed midday across central and eastern Nebraska.  A few of these storms produced downburst winds that blew down trees and large tree limbs in Saunders County and Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail and 40 mph winds were reported east-northeast of Yutan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree branch approximately 3 inches in diameter broke off in damaging winds 2 miles northeast of Memphis.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 50 to 60 mph wind gusts along with nickel size hail were reported approximately 5 miles northwest of Leshara.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms developed midday across central and eastern Nebraska.  A few of these storms produced downburst winds that blew down trees and large tree limbs in Saunders County and Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 1.50 inches of rain fell 1/2 mile west of Tecumseh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated cell within an area of thunderstorms produced quarter size hail and damaging winds that blew down tree limbs.
An isolated cell within an area of thunderstorms produced quarter size hail and damaging winds that blew down tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches and small hail was reported approximately 5 miles west of Stella.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved into the Central Plains on the 22nd.  A band of snow dumped over 10 inches of snow over a large area of northeast Nebraska.  Amounts of snow ranged from 1.8 inches in Lincoln to 17.6 inches in Bloomfield.  The largest amounts included 10 inches in Albion, 12.5 inches near Wakefield, 14 inches at Neligh, 14.6 inches at Norfolk, 15 inches at Stanton, and 16 inches at Pierce.  The heavy snow combined with strong winds created blizzard conditions across a large area of Northeastern Nebraska.  Roads were closed, and traffic came to a standstill on many highways.  Numerous power outages were reported as far south as Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The broadcast media relayed a public report of 17 inches of snow 3 miles southeast of St. James.Intense winds also produced blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated strong thunderstorm behind a cold front produced pea to nickel size hail across parts of Platte and Colfax County in the mid morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to penny size fell 9 miles north of Columbus.  Most of the stones were pea size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated strong thunderstorm behind a cold front produced pea to nickel size hail across parts of Platte and Colfax County in the mid morning.
An isolated strong thunderstorm behind a cold front produced pea to nickel size hail across parts of Platte and Colfax County in the mid morning.

Severe thunderstorms produced widespread high winds between Interstate 80 and Highway 6 on this Thursday night and Friday morning. During the mid to late-afternoon hours Thursday, scattered thunderstorms developed from the Nebraska Panhandle down into northeast Colorado. Storm mergers, and an increasing number of storms, resulted in the formation of a squall line by 700 PM CST. This line of storms crossed most of south central Nebraska between 10 PM CST on the 10th and 1 AM CST on the 11th. These storms produced widespread measured gusts between 58 and 70 mph along the entire way across south central Nebraska from the Interstate 80 corridor south to Highway 6. A couple unofficial weather stations measured higher wind speeds including 80 mph near Axtell and 78 near Norman, both in Kearney County. Despite the coverage and magnitude of the winds, damage was only reported in a few spots. Tree branches were snapped off in Minden, an irrigation pivot was overturned near Glenvil, a few trees were blown down in Central City, and six utility poles were blown down or snapped off near Harvard. There were reports in southern Adams County and western Clay County of zero visibility in blowing dust as the high winds from the outflow moved in before the rain fell. Farmers had been preparing the fields for planting. So, top soil was loose and available for easy lofting and transport. Behind the squall line, other scattered thunderstorms developed. The strongest storms moved east along the entire length of Interstate 80 from Lexington to York between 1230 AM and 330 AM CST. These storms were mostly sub-severe, but there was a single report of one inch hail in Kearney at 2 AM CST.||During the morning hours of Thursday, a strong southward-moving cold front crossed Nebraska and became stationary along the northern Kansas border. A low pressure center formed over Wyoming and, by early evening, began moving east along the front. Meanwhile, chilly high pressure moved from the Canadian Prairies into the Great Lakes region. A broad upper-level ridge was over the Plains with a trough along the East Coast and another trough advancing through the western states. MUCAPE was around 1500 J/kg immediately preceding the storms. These storms formed on the northeast fringe of an elevated mixed layer that was advecting into the region, ahead of the western trough. Deep layer shear was 50 kts and greater. This environment was more than sufficient for severe thunderstorms. The cold pool associated with the squall line was strong. The temperature at Lexington dropped 16 deg F in 20 minutes (76 to 60 deg F) as the gust front moved through, and 23 deg in 40 min (76 to 53 deg F).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

During the mid to late-afternoon hours Thursday, scattered thunderstorms developed from the Nebraska Panhandle down into northeast Colorado. Storm mergers, and an increasing number of storms, resulted in the formation of a squall line by 700 PM CST. This line of storms crossed most of south central Nebraska between 10 PM CST on the 10th and 1 AM CST on the 11th. These storms produced widespread measured gusts between 58 and 70 mph along the entire way across south central Nebraska from the Interstate 80 corridor south to Highway 6. A couple unofficial weather stations measured higher wind speeds including 80 mph near Axtell and 78 near Norman, both in Kearney County. Despite the coverage and magnitude of the winds, damage was only reported in a few spots. Tree branches were snapped off in Minden, an irrigation pivot was overturned near Glenvil, a few trees were blown down in Central City, and six utility poles were blown down or snapped off near Harvard. There were reports in southern Adams County and western Clay County of zero visibility in blowing dust as the high winds from the outflow moved in before the rain fell. Farmers had been preparing the fields for planting. So, top soil was loose and available for easy lofting and transport. Behind the squall line, other scattered thunderstorms developed. The strongest storms moved east along the entire length of Interstate 80 from Lexington to York between 1230 AM and 330 AM CST. These storms were mostly sub-severe, but there was a single report of one inch hail in Kearney at 2 AM CST.||During the morning hours of Thursday, a strong southward-moving cold front crossed Nebraska and became stationary along the northern Kansas border. A low pressure center formed over Wyoming and, by early evening, began moving east along the front. Meanwhile, chilly high pressure moved from the Canadian Prairies into the Great Lakes region. A broad upper-level ridge was over the Plains with a trough along the East Coast and another trough advancing through the western states. MUCAPE was around 1500 J/kg immediately preceding the storms. These storms formed on the northeast fringe of an elevated mixed layer that was advecting into the region, ahead of the western trough. Deep layer shear was 50 kts and greater. This environment was more than sufficient for severe thunderstorms. The cold pool associated with the squall line was strong. The temperature at Lexington dropped 16 deg F in 20 minutes (76 to 60 deg F) as the gust front moved through, and 23 deg in 40 min (76 to 53 deg F).

Severe thunderstorms produced widespread high winds between Interstate 80 and Highway 6 on this Thursday night and Friday morning. During the mid to late-afternoon hours Thursday, scattered thunderstorms developed from the Nebraska Panhandle down into northeast Colorado. Storm mergers, and an increasing number of storms, resulted in the formation of a squall line by 700 PM CST. This line of storms crossed most of south central Nebraska between 10 PM CST on the 10th and 1 AM CST on the 11th. These storms produced widespread measured gusts between 58 and 70 mph along the entire way across south central Nebraska from the Interstate 80 corridor south to Highway 6. A couple unofficial weather stations measured higher wind speeds including 80 mph near Axtell and 78 near Norman, both in Kearney County. Despite the coverage and magnitude of the winds, damage was only reported in a few spots. Tree branches were snapped off in Minden, an irrigation pivot was overturned near Glenvil, a few trees were blown down in Central City, and six utility poles were blown down or snapped off near Harvard. There were reports in southern Adams County and western Clay County of zero visibility in blowing dust as the high winds from the outflow moved in before the rain fell. Farmers had been preparing the fields for planting. So, top soil was loose and available for easy lofting and transport. Behind the squall line, other scattered thunderstorms developed. The strongest storms moved east along the entire length of Interstate 80 from Lexington to York between 1230 AM and 330 AM CST. During a roughly 36-hour period between the morning of Saturday the 1st and the evening of Sunday the 2nd, South Central Nebraska observed off-and-on rounds of snow, with cumulative totals generally ranging from 1-4 in most counties along/east of Highway 281, to generally 3-5 in most counties west of Highway 281. A couple of exceptions to this lower east/higher west generalization included Greeley and Polk counties, which (like many western areas) picked up a solid 3-5. Per NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest official totals featured: 5.5 in Holdrege, 5.1 near Shelby, 5.0 in Beaver City, 4.9 at Kearney, 4.7 in Cambridge and 4.5 in Ord. While this was not a true blizzard by any means, occasional visibility reductions to one-half mile or less were noted, especially on the afternoon and evening of the 1st, when sustained north winds of 20-25 MPH and frequent gusts of 30-40 MPH were most prevalent. ||Backing up in time a bit, snow was not the only story associated with this long-duration event, as it actually started off as a significant late-autumn rain-producer. In fact, most of the 24-county area measured 1-2 of soaking rain between the afternoon of Nov. 30th and the morning of the 1st, before precipitation finally switched over to snow. Due in part to much of the initial precipitation falling as rain, along with the fact that a significant batch of mid-level dry air got wrapped into the heart of the parent low pressure system, snowfall totals in most of the area fell well-short of expectations (many places only received about half of what was originally forecasted). In the mid-upper levels, the main driving force was a strong, slow-moving low pressure system that tracked from south central KS on the morning of the 1st to the IA/MO border area by the morning of the 2nd before weakening and shearing-out eastward into the Midwest. The intermittent areas of snow that persisted across the local area through most of the 1st-2nd were supported by weaker lobes of energy rotating around the backside of the parent system. At the surface, the associated low pressure center peaked in intensity around 988 millibars over eastern KS on the 1st, thus driving the modestly-strong north winds over Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 2.4 inches occurred at WFO Hastings, NE. North wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph developed the afternoon of December 1st, lasting into the overnight. This sent visibility down to one mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm began in the afternoon hours on December 26th, continuing into the early morning hours on December 28th across the eastern half of western and north central Nebraska. Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph occurred during the peak of the storm. This created low visibility and significant blowing and drifting. Blizzard conditions were observed in some areas. Some roads, including Interstate 80 west of North Platte became closed on December 27th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 12 inches across much of the area with the higher amounts occurring across Thomas, Blaine, Loup, and Custer Counties.

This created low visibility and significant blowing and drifting.

Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph occurred beginning during the early morning hours, daytime and evening hours on December 27th into the early morning hours on December 28th. This created low visibility of a quarter mile or less at times and significant blowing and drifting. Some county roads became closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm began in the afternoon hours on December 26th, continuing into the early morning hours on December 28th across the eastern half of western and north central Nebraska. Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph occurred during the peak of the storm. This created low visibility and significant blowing and drifting. Blizzard conditions were observed in some areas. Some roads, including Interstate 80 west of North Platte became closed on December 27th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 12 inches across much of the area with the higher amounts occurring across Thomas, Blaine, Loup, and Custer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative observer in O'Neill reported 6 inches of snowfall. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 8 inches across Holt County. Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph occurred beginning during the early morning hours, daytime and evening hours on December 27th into the early morning hours on December 28th. This created low visibility and significant blowing and drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a roughly 36-hour period between the morning of Saturday the 1st and the evening of Sunday the 2nd, South Central Nebraska observed off-and-on rounds of snow, with cumulative totals generally ranging from 1-4 in most counties along/east of Highway 281, to generally 3-5 in most counties west of Highway 281. A couple of exceptions to this lower east/higher west generalization included Greeley and Polk counties, which (like many western areas) picked up a solid 3-5. Per NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest official totals featured: 5.5 in Holdrege, 5.1 near Shelby, 5.0 in Beaver City, 4.9 at Kearney, 4.7 in Cambridge and 4.5 in Ord. While this was not a true blizzard by any means, occasional visibility reductions to one-half mile or less were noted, especially on the afternoon and evening of the 1st, when sustained north winds of 20-25 MPH and frequent gusts of 30-40 MPH were most prevalent. ||Backing up in time a bit, snow was not the only story associated with this long-duration event, as it actually started off as a significant late-autumn rain-producer. In fact, most of the 24-county area measured 1-2 of soaking rain between the afternoon of Nov. 30th and the morning of the 1st, before precipitation finally switched over to snow. Due in part to much of the initial precipitation falling as rain, along with the fact that a significant batch of mid-level dry air got wrapped into the heart of the parent low pressure system, snowfall totals in most of the area fell well-short of expectations (many places only received about half of what was originally forecasted). In the mid-upper levels, the main driving force was a strong, slow-moving low pressure system that tracked from south central KS on the morning of the 1st to the IA/MO border area by the morning of the 2nd before weakening and shearing-out eastward into the Midwest. The intermittent areas of snow that persisted across the local area through most of the 1st-2nd were supported by weaker lobes of energy rotating around the backside of the parent system. At the surface, the associated low pressure center peaked in intensity around 988 millibars over eastern KS on the 1st, thus driving the modestly-strong north winds over Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 2.3 inches occurred at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. North wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph developed the afternoon of December 1st, lasting into the overnight. This sent visibility down to one mile or less at times.

Polk also received 3.0 inches of snow. North wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph developed the afternoon of December 1st, lasting into the overnight.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 2.8 inches occurred 4 miles southwest of Blue Hill. North wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph developed the afternoon of December 1st, lasting into the overnight. This sent visibility down to one mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a roughly 36-hour period between the morning of Saturday the 1st and the evening of Sunday the 2nd, South Central Nebraska observed off-and-on rounds of snow, with cumulative totals generally ranging from 1-4 in most counties along/east of Highway 281, to generally 3-5 in most counties west of Highway 281. A couple of exceptions to this lower east/higher west generalization included Greeley and Polk counties, which (like many western areas) picked up a solid 3-5. Per NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest official totals featured: 5.5 in Holdrege, 5.1 near Shelby, 5.0 in Beaver City, 4.9 at Kearney, 4.7 in Cambridge and 4.5 in Ord. While this was not a true blizzard by any means, occasional visibility reductions to one-half mile or less were noted, especially on the afternoon and evening of the 1st, when sustained north winds of 20-25 MPH and frequent gusts of 30-40 MPH were most prevalent. ||Backing up in time a bit, snow was not the only story associated with this long-duration event, as it actually started off as a significant late-autumn rain-producer. In fact, most of the 24-county area measured 1-2 of soaking rain between the afternoon of Nov. 30th and the morning of the 1st, before precipitation finally switched over to snow. Due in part to much of the initial precipitation falling as rain, along with the fact that a significant batch of mid-level dry air got wrapped into the heart of the parent low pressure system, snowfall totals in most of the area fell well-short of expectations (many places only received about half of what was originally forecasted). In the mid-upper levels, the main driving force was a strong, slow-moving low pressure system that tracked from south central KS on the morning of the 1st to the IA/MO border area by the morning of the 2nd before weakening and shearing-out eastward into the Midwest. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first true blizzard of the 2018-19 winter season struck much of the northwest half of South Central Nebraska on this Thursday, causing not only many of the usual winter storm disruptions, but also snarling post-Christmas travel. Among various other impacts, Interstate 80 was initially closed from Lexington westward to North Platte, but with this closure eventually creeping as far east as Grand Island to accommodate traffic back-up. There was a fairly sharp divide in snow totals and storm impacts across the area, as locations generally northwest of an Alma-Kearney-Fullerton line received the highest amounts of 4-9, and in turn, experienced the most severe blizzard conditions. Amounts dropped off steadily to the southeast of that line, with places such as Osceola, Grand Island, Hastings and Red Cloud only seeing 1-2. Even farther southeast, counties such as Thayer/Fillmore/Nuckolls barely received a dusting. Per NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest official totals featured: 8.5 in Greeley, 8 in Edison, 7.9 in Loup City, 7.2 six miles southeast of Lexington and 7 in Arcadia, Cambridge and Wilsonville. In the Tri Cities, Kearney led the way with 3.8, while Grand Island and Hastings only measured 1.9. Winds were certainly a major factor, as much of the 27th featured sustained north-northwest speeds of 25-35 MPH and frequent gusts of 40-50 MPH, resulting in considerable blowing/drifting snow and reducing visibilities to around 1/4 mile for several hours in the Blizzard Warning area. ||Breaking down event timing, and initially backing up a bit, this storm started off very similar to the one four weeks prior, and with something fairly unusual for this time of year: soaking winter rain! In fact, Wednesday the 26th featured widespread rainfall of 1-2, including daily records in Grand Island (1.42) and Hastings (1.41). As colder air invaded western counties late that night, rain quickly changed to snow, with blizzard conditions already in full force by sunrise on the 27th in places such as Kearney, Lexington and Ord. Even before snow impacts kicked into high gear, the initially rain-wettened roads became slick due to a flash freeze in the falling temperatures. For the hardest-hit northern/western counties, the bulk of snow occurred during the early-mid morning, while areas farther south/east nearer the Highway 281 corridor (including Grand Island/Hastings) observed the majority of snow and occasional white-out conditions during the late-morning through mid-afternoon. Although blowing/drifting snow persisted in many areas well into the night, South Central Nebraska saw a pronounced break from falling snow through the late afternoon-evening, before one last batch of light snow (accumulation largely under one-half inch) flared up on the morning of the 28th. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was a powerful, large scale trough, with its center tracking from NM on the 26th, to western KS by daybreak on the 27th, then northward across eastern NE into MN during the afternoon and evening. The associated strong surface low pressure system started the morning of the 27th over central Kansas (pressure around 996 millibars), before steadily marching to around Kansas City by mid-day and the IA/MN border by evening. On a climate-related side note, this event put an exclamation point on an incredibly wet December, as most of South Central Nebraska received between 2.50-4.50 of precipitation for the month, or generally 300-600% of normal! For most official stations, it was a Top-3 wettest December on record, and was the outright-wettest at places such as Osceola.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall of 1.9 inches was measured at the NWS office just north of Hastings. Wind gusts frequently reached 35 to 45 mph during the snow, frequently bringing visibility down to a half mile or less.

In the Tri Cities, Kearney led the way with 3.8, while Grand Island and Hastings only measured 1.9. Winds were certainly a major factor, as much of the 27th featured sustained north-northwest speeds of 25-35 MPH and frequent gusts of 40-50 MPH, resulting in considerable blowing/drifting snow and reducing visibilities to around 1/4 mile for several hours in the Blizzard Warning area. ||Breaking down event timing, and initially backing up a bit, this storm started off very similar to the one four weeks prior, and with something fairly unusual for this time of year: soaking winter rain! In fact, Wednesday the 26th featured widespread rainfall of 1-2, including daily records in Grand Island (1.42) and Hastings (1.41). As colder air invaded western counties late that night, rain quickly changed to snow, with blizzard conditions already in full force by sunrise on the 27th in places such as Kearney, Lexington and Ord. Even before snow impacts kicked into high gear, the initially rain-wettened roads became slick due to a flash freeze in the falling temperatures. For the hardest-hit northern/western counties, the bulk of snow occurred during the early-mid morning, while areas farther south/east nearer the Highway 281 corridor (including Grand Island/Hastings) observed the majority of snow and occasional white-out conditions during the late-morning through mid-afternoon. Although blowing/drifting snow persisted in many areas well into the night, South Central Nebraska saw a pronounced break from falling snow through the late afternoon-evening, before one last batch of light snow (accumulation largely under one-half inch) flared up on the morning of the 28th. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was a powerful, large scale trough, with its center tracking from NM on the 26th, to western KS by daybreak on the 27th, then northward across eastern NE into MN during the afternoon and evening. The associated strong surface low pressure system started the morning of the 27th over central Kansas (pressure around 996 millibars), before steadily marching to around Kansas City by mid-day and the IA/MN border by evening. On a climate-related side note, this event put an exclamation point on an incredibly wet December, as most of South Central Nebraska received between 2.50-4.50 of precipitation for the month, or generally 300-600% of normal! For most official stations, it was a Top-3 wettest December on record, and was the outright-wettest at places such as Osceola.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall of 6.3 inches was measured eight miles south of Elwood, reported by a cooperative observer. Wind gusts frequently reached 40 to 50 mph during the snow, frequently bringing visibility down to a quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snowfall beginning in the early morning hours on December 1st, continuing into the early morning hours on December 2nd across most of western and north central Nebraska. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 14 inches across much of the area with the higher amounts occurring across north central into west central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General public located 16 miles southwest of Mullen reported 11 inches of snowfall. Moderate to heavy snow occurred during the morning into the afternoon hours on December 1st. North winds of 15 to 25 mph caused areas of blowing and drifting snow with visibility frequently reduced to a half mile. Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 12 inches across Hooker County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved across Sidney producing hail measured up to the size of quarters.

Heavy snow fell across portions of Scotts Bluff and Banner Counties on the evening of the 16th ending in the evening of the 17th. Snowfall of over six inches reported across the cities of Gering and Melbeta while eight inches of snowfall was reported at the Banner County Sheriff's Office in Harrisburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Banner County Sheriff's Office measured eight inches of snowfall at their office in downtown Harrisburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought wide spread precipitation to Southwest Nebraska, beginning on the 26th. The precipitation began as rain then transitioned to snow during the night of the 26th. Winds became strong as the rain changed over to snow the night of the 26th. This caused blizzard conditions to occur in the snowfall. The blizzard conditions swept northeast into Nebraska, then ended as the snowfall shifted east.  The highest snowfall amount reported was 6.5 in Culbertson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions were reported in Culbertson during the storm.  Snowfall amounts ranged from around an inch in the western part of the county to six and a half inches in the east.
